OF THE
COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
PASSED AT THE REGULAR SESSION OF THE GENERAL
ASSEMBLY , WHICH WAS BEGUN IN THE CITY OF

CHAPTER 1 .
AN ACT to empower and direct the Commissioner of Agricul-
ture, Labor, and Statistics , to establish and promulgate official
standard grades for tobacco in green order ; to provide for the
inspection , grading and marketing of tobacco in green order ,
and to prohibit violations of this Act and to prescribe penal-
ties , and to fix a charge of not to exceed one- half of one cent
per hundred pounds to defray expenses of administering this
Act and to be paid by the purchaser .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
1. For the purpose of this Act the terms :
(a) "Tobacco in green order " means any tobacco
in green order sold in Kentucky .
(b ) " Seller " means any association , corporation ,
warehouseman , and/or person and/or his or its rep-
resentative offering for sale or selling tobacco in
green order in this State .
(c) " Commissioner ' means the Commissioner
of Agriculture , Labor and Statistics .
2. The Commissioner is hereby empowered
and directed in his discretion :
(a) To establish and promulgate from time to
time official standard grades for any kinds of to-

Section 864. Withdrawals . A member may with-
draw his or her unpledged shares at any time by
giving thirty days ' written notice of his or her
desire to do so . Upon the filing of such written ap-
plication to withdraw , the association shall number
and file the same in the order received and shall ,
after thirty days from the receipt of such applica-
tion to withdraw , either pay the holder the value
thereof , in part or in full as requested , or apply at
least one-half of the receipts of the association as
hereinafter provided from its shareholders and bor-
rowers to the withdrawal of such shares in numer-
ical order. Shareholders filing written application
for the withdrawal of their shares shall remain
shareholders until paid , and shall not become
creditors .
Provided , further that an association shall not be

half of the net weekly receipts of such association
after the payment of current dividends , and after
making provision for anticipated dividends and the

rowed , and after paying administration expenses of
the association , taxes , insurance and repairs on
property owned , municipal assessments , and any
other necessary disbursements .

for a withdrawal of more than five hundred
($500.00 ) dollars , he shall be paid $ 500.00 in order

Section 865a . Loans ; How Secured ; Penalties
and Failure to Offer Good Security . For every loan
made a note secured by first mortgage on real estate
shall be given accompanied by a transfer and pledge

shall be held by the corporation as collateral secu-
rity for the performance of the conditions of said

and the interest and premiums , if any , upon the
loan , together with all fines , upon payments in
arrears until said shares shall reach the value fixed
by articles of incorporation or the by-laws or said
loan is otherwise canceled and discharged .
Paid up , installment and other shares without
other security may , at the discretion of the direc
tors , be pledged as security for loans to an amount
not exceeding 80 per cent of their value , as de-
termined at the time of application for said loan .

56 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That Section 4134 of Kentucky Statutes ( Car-
roll's 1930 Edition ) relating to bonds of sheriffs
and sureties thereon , be amended and re -enacted , so
tha said section , when amended and re-enacted , shall
read as follows , to -wit :
4134. The county court may require the sheriff
to give an additional bond or bonds , with good sure-
ty , to be approved by the county court whenever it
may deem the interest of the state , or county de-
mands ; and the sureties on all the bonds executed by
the sheriff shall be jointly and severally liable for
any default of the sheriff during the year in which
said bond may be executed , whether the liability
accrues before or after the execution of such bond
or bonds : Provided , that a surety on any bond exe-
cuted by the sheriff shall not in any event be liable
for any act or default of the sheriff occurring in
any calendar year other than that within which the
bond or bonds shall have been executed ; and pro-
vided, further that neither the sheriff nor a surety
or sureties on any bond or bonds executed by the
sheriff shall be liable for any act or default of the
sheriff unless the act or default of the sheriff giving
rise to a claim upon such bond shall have been
given to the surety or sureties by the State Auditor ,
the judge of the county court the county attorney or
other person asserting such claim ninety days after
such discovery or at the latest within the period of
one year from and after the end of the year within
which such bond or bonds shall have been executed .
Whereas , many sheriffs are required to execute
bonds within a short time , and in many instances
sureties are unwilling to execute such bonds until
this statute as amended shall have gone into effect ,
i

classification.title: AN ACT to amend and re-enact Section 4664 of Kentucky Statutes (Carroll's 1930 Edition) relating to bonds; discharge of sureties thereon and providing for penalties for failure to execute good and sufficient bonds; and declaring an emergency to exist.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 4664 of the Kentucky Statutes concerning the discharge of sureties on bonds and penalties for failure to execute good and sufficient bonds. It specifies that if a new bond is not provided as required, the party moved against will be removed from office, have their powers revoked, or be deprived of their right to act in their trust. The court is to make orders for the protection of the surety and the benefit of the state or trust. An emergency is declared, making the act effective immediately upon passage and approval.

The language is general and applies to all individuals holding office or acting in a trust capacity. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Statutes Section 186d-1 concerning the release of sureties on bonds for public officials and fiduciaries. It outlines the process for a surety to be released from future liability by filing a motion and notice, and specifies a 30-day period for a new bond to be executed or for the principal to be removed from office. classification.title: An Act to amend Section 186d-1 of Kentucky Statutes (Carroll's 1930 Edition) relating to bonds of officers and fiduciaries and sureties thereon and funds and their deposits secured by such bonds.
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Statutes Section 186d-1, clarifying that official and fiduciary bonds serve as a covenant to the Commonwealth for the faithful discharge of duties. It specifies that recovery against a surety is limited to the bond's penal sum, which is to be fixed by the approving officer. For fiduciaries, the bond must be at least the estimated value of the estate. The approving officer may also increase the bond's penal sum or require a renewal if deemed in the obligee's interest.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the legal requirements and limitations of bonds for public officials and fiduciaries. It specifies the nature of the bond as a covenant, the limit of liability for sureties to the penal sum, and the process for setting and potentially increasing this sum. There is no mention of race, and the provisions apply universally to all individuals holding public office or acting as fiduciaries, regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

7. Continuous Financial Planning . The State
Budget Officer shall have in continuous process of
preparation and revision , in the light of his direct
studies of the operations , plans , and needs of budget
units and of the existing and prospective sources of
income , a tentative budget report for the next en-
suing biennium for which a budget report is re-
quired to be prepared . Upon receipt of the esti-
mates of the several budget units , he shall check

and shall make such further inquiries and investi-
gations and revise his tentative budget report as he
deems warranted . Such tentative budget report ,
when approved by the Board of Finance , shall be
certified to the Governor , together with the budget
statements provided in Section 6.

classification.title: Appropriation of Preceding Year Continued Under Certain Conditions
classification.summary: This section outlines a procedure for continuing appropriations from the previous fiscal year if the General Assembly fails to make new appropriations or if the Governor vetoes essential appropriations. It specifies that the amount appropriated in the preceding year, excluding extraordinary expenses and capital outlays, will be deemed appropriated and available until a new appropriation is made. The Board of Finance is tasked with determining the proportion of appropriations to be continued if they cover multiple budget classes. These continued appropriations are to be included in the budget and made available through allotments.

§ 18. Allotments to Govern Expenditures : Per-
missible Transfers Within Allotments . The heads of
the budget units shall hold to the work plans and
detailed expenditure estimates as authorized and
approved on advices of allotments , except as devia-
tions therefrom are made necessary by changes in
conditions of operations , to meet unforseen con-
. tingencies , and to correct errors . In no case shall
obligations be incurred or expenditures made in
excess of the total amounts allotted . Transfers
from an allowance for one budget class to an allow-
ance for another budget class within the same bud-
get unit , may be authorized by the administrative
head of the budget unit to which the allotment was
made , if approved by the Board of Finance , and
provided that the Auditor of Public Accounts and
the State Budget Officer shall be promptly advised
of all such transfers thus authorized .
$ 19. Interpretation of Items in Appropriation
Acts . All questions which may arise as to the mean-
ing of items specified in any appropriation act or in
the budget , shall be decided by the Board of Finance
in accordance with the detailed estimates or other
information embodied in the budget report as con-
sidered by the General Assembly, and the decision
of the Board shall be final and conclusive .
§ 20. Head of any Budget Unit May Appeal to
Board of Finance : When . The head of any unit ag-
grieved by any decision of the State Budget Officer
as to the amount of any allotment for a specified
period or as to the purposes for which monies al-
lotted may be expended , may appeal therefrom to
the Board of Finance , and the decision of such
Board shall be final and conclusive .

classification.title: Allotments to Govern Expenditures : Permissible Transfers Within Allotments
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for managing budget allotments for state agencies. It details how agency heads must adhere to approved work plans and expenditure estimates, with provisions for deviations due to changed conditions or unforeseen contingencies. It also specifies the process for transferring funds between budget classes within the same agency, requiring authorization from the administrative head and approval from the Board of Finance, with notification to the Auditor of Public Accounts and the State Budget Officer. Additionally, it clarifies that the Board of Finance has the final say on the interpretation of appropriation act items and that agency heads can appeal decisions of the State Budget Officer to the Board of Finance.

classification.title: ARTICLE IV-CONTROL OF THE INCURRING OF OBLIGATIONS
classification.summary: This article outlines the procedures for incurring and evidencing financial obligations for the Commonwealth of Kentucky. It mandates that commitments, except for specific legislative expenses, salaries of elected officials, and special grants, must be supported by commitment statements filed with the State Budget Officer. These statements can be advice of employment for personal services or copies of purchase orders/contracts detailing work, services, commodities, and costs. If a purchase order or contract is not feasible, a written requisition to the Auditor of Public Accounts is required.

cial form of statement of indebtedness or payroll

$ 2 . Pre-Audit of Statements of Indebtedness .
The State Budget Officer shall prescribe the forms
on which statements of indebtedness of every kind ,
including indebtedness for the salaries of officers
and employees , shall be presented , and it shall be
the duty of the Auditor to examine all such state-
ments so presented and if found correct , to file them
and issue warrants to the State Treasurer . All
claims against the State shall be paid by the State
Treasurer on the warrants of the Auditor of Public
Accounts . Payments may be made direct by the
State Treasurer to the persons entitled to receive
them , by mail or otherwise , or through the head of
the budget units which incurred the expenditures .
The Auditor of Public Accounts shall not certify
any claim whatsoever , to the State Treasure until
he is satisfied that to meet said claim , there is a
sufficient balance of the sum appropriated to the
budget unit from which such claim may be paid .

§ 1. Periodic and Special Audits ; Filing of Audit

and Examiner to audit the accounts , records and
transactions of the budget units , not less frequently

ing State aid or having responsibility for handling
any State funds . Copies of the audit reports shall
be filed with the departments , institutions , boards ,
and other public offices and agencies audited , and a
copy of each report shall be filed with the Gover-
nor . He shall make special audits and investigations
at such times as may be required by the Governor ,
and shall audit periodically the revenue collections
of all kinds accruing to the Commonwealth , to ascer-
tain whether the revenue due and owing to the Com-
monwealth is being properly and completely col-
lected , and if he shall find from said audit or audits
that the collections are not being satisfactorily
made , he shall report that fact to the authority
whose duty it is to make such collections . All audit
reports of the examiners shall be public documents
to which taxpayers shall have access .
§ 2. Audits of the Accounts of the Auditor and
the Treasurer . The State Inspector and Examiner
shall audit the accounts of the Auditor of Public
Accounts and of the Treasurer not less frequently
than once each month .

CHAPTER 28 .
AN ACT to assist disabled ex-service men and women of the
World War and veterans of other wars in obtaining from the
Federal Government the relief authorized by Acts of Congress ,
and continuing in existence for that purpose the Kentucky
Disabled Ex -Service Men's Board and appropriating funds
therefor ; providing maintenance of an office and stenographic
help to carry on the work of said Board .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That the Kentucky Disabled Ex - Service
Men's Board , created by Act approved February
27 , 1922 , and recreated by Acts of the General As-
sembly approved March 21 , 1924 , February 24 , 1926,
February 16 , 1928 , March 11 , 1930 , and by an Act of
the General Assembly 1932 , be continued in exist-
ence for an additional period of two ( 2 ) years for
the purpose of assisting ex- service men and women
of the World War and their dependents , and vet-
erans of other wars and their dependents who are
residents of Kentucky , and who are or may be en-
titled to aid from the United States Government
pursuant to federal legislation for the benefit of vet-
erans and their dependents , in preparing and pre-
senting their said claims to the proper federal agen-
cies and authorities and in securing and expediting
action thereon ; to relieve and prevent congestion
and delay in the proper consideration , rating and
final adjudication of said claims and to , as far as
possible , cause every disabled veteran and the de-
pendents of every disabled veteran to receive
promptly and regularly the maximum relief and
benefits to which they are entitled under the federal
law .
§ 2. Said Kentucky Disabled Ex- Service Men's
Board shall, during the time for which its existence

classification.summary: This act continues the existence of the Kentucky Disabled Ex-Service Men's Board for an additional two years. The board's purpose is to assist disabled ex-service men and women of the World War and veterans of other wars, along with their dependents, who are residents of Kentucky, in obtaining federal relief and benefits. It aims to help them prepare and present claims to federal agencies, expedite action on these claims, and ensure prompt and regular receipt of maximum entitled relief.

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 1 of Chapter 28 of the Acts of 1934, relating to the Kentucky Disabled Ex-Service Men's Board.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act to specify the composition of the Kentucky Disabled Ex-Service Men's Board. The board will consist of the Lieutenant Governor, the State Commander of the American Legion, and a third member elected by the first two. The act also defines 'veterans' and 'ex-service men and women' as individuals who served on active duty in the U.S. Army, Navy, or Marine Corps with an honorable discharge. The board is empowered to elect a secretary and assistants to manage claims for disabled veterans and their dependents with the U.S. Veterans' Administration and other federal agencies. Salaries and travel expenses for the secretary and assistants are to be paid.

classification.title: An Act to Create a State Planning Board
classification.summary: This act establishes a State Planning Board composed of nine members. Five members are ex-officio representatives from the State Highway Commission, State Board of Health, State Board of Education, University of Kentucky, and Department of Public Welfare. The Governor appoints the remaining four members and designates a chairman. The Board is tasked with coordinating with the United States Government or its agencies on planning matters and may receive funding from the US Government. Offices are to be maintained in Frankfort.

There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'GenAssemb 1934.pdf' and the structure of the text which appears to be an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the text clearly defines the purpose and structure of the board without any racial implications. The category is 'other' as the statute's primary function is administrative and organizational, not directly related to the typical categories of Jim Crow laws.

classification.title: An Act relating to the State Planning Board and its powers and duties.
classification.summary: This act establishes and defines the powers and duties of the State Planning Board. It outlines the board's responsibilities in collecting and publishing information on welfare problems, making recommendations to the General Assembly, and entering lands for surveys. The act also mandates that state departments and officials consult the Planning Board for recommendations on legislative or executive approvals of plans, appropriations for public improvements, and changes in the use or disposition of state-owned real property. The board is tasked with preparing and submitting plans for state highways and parkways to the General Assembly.
classification.reasoning: The statute text provided describes the establishment and functions of a State Planning Board. Its duties include collecting information on welfare problems, promoting state planning, and developing plans for highways and parkways. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it is presented as a chapter from a legislative session (GenAssemb 1934.pdf).

such highways and parkways . Such plans and reg-
ulations shall be designated to promote health ,

commodation of local traffic , cross traffic and traffic
to and from the abutting frontages ; to establish

Such plans and regulations when approved by the

ment program of major state improvement projects .
The various state departments and officials shall
prepare and submit to such Planning Board their
proposals for major projects . It shall be the duty
of such Planning Board to co -ordinate such plans
and proposals with each other and with the general
plans of such Board , and as a result to submit to the
Governor and General Assembly a report at least
once every two years , showing such Board's recom-
mendations and program for improvement projects .

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 4114i-13, Kentucky Statutes, 1930 Edition, relating to the State Tax Commission.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 4114i-13 of the Kentucky Statutes, 1930 Edition. It allows the State Tax Commission to establish rules for data and information exchange with other states and the federal government under reciprocal agreements. It also reiterates the prohibition against divulging information acquired by tax officials regarding company affairs or tax schedules, with penalties for violations, but with exceptions for prosecutions related to false tax reports or other tax law infractions.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the administrative procedures and confidentiality of information held by the State Tax Commission. It allows for reciprocal data sharing between states and the federal government and reinforces penalties for divulging sensitive tax information. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly create racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

128 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
The term "penal or correctional institution "
shall include any prison , penitentiary , reformatory ,
workhouse , jail or other institution wholly tax- sup-
ported and maintained for the incarceration or im-
prisonment of persons convicted of an offense
against the laws of the United States , any state or
its political subdivisions, but shall not include insti-
tutions maintained under the jurisdiction of private
charitable or religious organizations .
The term " prison industry " as used herein shall
mean any industry or trade in whatever manner op-
erated for the mining , manufacture , production
and/or distribution of products , in which are em-
ployed inmates of penal or correctional institutions
or other persons who at the time of employment
are serving a sentence imposed by a court of the
United States or any state : Provided , that no agri-
cultural activities not a part of any trade or indus-
try shall be considered a prison industry as herein
defined .
"Products " shall mean all goods , wares , merchan-
dise and minerals manufactured , produced or mined
in whole or in part by prison industry for other than
State use .
ARTICLE II
Hours of Labor . The hours of labor in prison in-
dustries shall be not more than those prescribed by
the applicable Code adopted under the laws of the
United States governing each particular industry ,
but in no case shall any inmate be required or per-
mitted to work in any prison industry more than
forty hours in any one week .
ARTICLE III
The hours of operation of productive machinery
shall be not more than is prescribed in the code of
the competing private domestic industry .

ARTICLE IV
Child Labor. No person under the age of sixteen
years shall be employed in any prison industry . No
person under the age of eighteen years shall be em-
ployed at operations or occupations which are haz-
ardous in nature or dangerous to health .
ARTICLE V
Section A. Prison products , when sold by the
prison or through a contractor, whether sold direct
or through any agency , shall be sold not lower than
the fair current price prevailing in the market in
which the product is customarily sold to whole-
salers , retailers , or consumers as the case may be .

agency thereof shall enter into any contract for the
labor of prisoners which does not insure a return
from the contractor to the state or its political sub-
division and/or the prisoner of an amount equal in
value to the cost per unit of product for labor and
overhead necessarily paid in competing domestic
private industry on the comparable product : Pro-
vided , that the imposition or collection of such
amounts or charges shall not be such as will require
the sale of prison products at a higher price than
specified in Section A hereof.

It specifies that probation may be granted if the court is satisfied that the defendant is unlikely to re-offend and that the public good does not require the imposition of the full penalty. The act also clarifies that it does not affect laws concerning juvenile delinquents and that detention in juvenile institutions is not considered imprisonment. Furthermore, it allows the Commonwealth's Attorney to appeal probation judgments.

It specifies which crimes are eligible for probation and outlines the conditions under which it can be granted or revoked. Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That Chapter 50 of the Acts of the General As-
sembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky , as
amended and re - enacted at the 1924 session , being
Section 794 of Baldwin's Kentucky Statutes , Car-
roll's 1930 Edition , be and the same is hereby re-
pealed and re -enacted , so that as re-enacted said
law shall read as follows :
"Any person who shall recklessly throw any
stone , stick , club or other missle at or into , or shoot
at or in to any engine of any railroad train in this
state , or any car attached thereto , or any station ,
station platform or waiting room , on or in which
engine , car , station , station platform or waiting
room where there may be any passenger or other
person , or at or into any automobile or other motor
vehicle , stationary or traveling upon any public
highway in this Commonwealth , shall be guilty of a
misdemeanor , and upon conviction thereof shall be
fined not less than one hundred nor more than five
hundred dollars , or imprisoned in the county jail
for not less than six months nor more than twelve ;
and if such stick , stone , club or missle so thrown
or shot so fired was calculated to produce death or
great bodily harm, and any person or passenger on
or in said engine , car attached thereto , station , sta-
tion platform or waiting room , automobile or motor
vehicle , shall be injured or wounded , said person
so throwing same or shooting shall be deemed guilty
of a felony and upon conviction thereof shall be im-
prisoned in the penitentiary not less than one or
more than five years ; and , should death ensue from
such throwing or shooting within one year there-
after , the person guilty of same as herein provided
shall be deemed guilty of murder .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That section one thousand three hundred
twenty-one, Carroll's Kentucky Statutes , One
thousand nine hundred thirty edition , be and the
same is amended and re - enacted and as amended
and re-enacted shall read as follows :
No work or business shall be done on the Sab-
bath day , except the ordinary household offices , or
other work of necessity or charity , or work required
in the maintenance or operation of a ferry , skiff ,
steamboat or steam or street railroad , telegraph and
telephone companies ; public service or public utility
plants or systems , or taxi . If any person on the
Sabbath day shall himself be found at his own , or
at any other trade or calling or shall employ his
apprentices , or other person , in labor or other busi-
ness , whether the same be for profit or amusement ,
unless such as is permitted above , he shall be fined
not less than two nor more than fifty dollars for
each offense . Every person or apprentice so em-
ployed shall be deemed a separate offense . Persons
who are members of a religious society who observe
as a Sabbath any other day in the week than Sun-
day, shall not be liable to the penalty prescribed in
this section , if they observe as a Sabbath one day in
each seven , as herein provided . Provided that ama-
teur sports , athletic games , operation of moving pic-
ture show , chautauqua , filling stations , or opera shall
not be construed a work , labor , trade, business or
calling within the meaning of this section .
§ 2. All laws or parts of laws in conflict with
this act are hereby repealed .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

The militia of this State shall be divided into three
classes :
(a ) Part of the organized militia to be known
as the " Kentucky National Guard . "
(b ) Another part of the organized militia to be
known as the " Active Militia . " "
(c ) The reserve militia , consisting of all other
persons liable to military service .
The Governor shall be the Commander -in - Chief
of all three classes of the Militia .
2. GOVERNOR TO ORGANIZE ACTIVE MILITIA :
The Governor of the Commonwealth shall , and
he is hereby authorized to organize the Active
Militia in each County of the State , to consist of not
less than one nor more than five such militiamen for
each thousand of population , which number shall
upon order of the Governor , be apportioned to each
County . The Governor shall appoint and com-
mission one sober , discreet and qualified person , not
less than thirty years of age , in each County in the
State , to be the Senior Officer of the Active Militia
therein . The rank of the officer so appointed , to be
governed by the number of Active Militiamen ap-
portioned to the County.
§ 3. SELECTION OF PERSONNEL :
The officer in each County chosen and commis-
sioned by the Governor , together with the County
Judge , and the Sheriff shall compose a Board to
select the personnel for the unit or units of the
Active Militia therein. No person shall be accepted
as a member of the Active Militia who shall not have

classification.summary: This statute outlines the qualifications, appointment, enlistment, and discharge procedures for members of the Active Militia in Kentucky. It specifies age, citizenship, moral character, and physical condition requirements for applicants. Officers are appointed by the Governor, and enlisted men are enlisted by Company Commanders. Discharges can be ordered by the Governor or requested by two members of the County Board. Enlistments are for two years. All members must take oaths similar to those required for the National Guard and the oath of office prescribed by the Kentucky Constitution.

$ 9. GOVERNOR MAY DISBAND AND REORGANIZE THE
ACTIVE MILITIA :
The Governor shall have power to alter , divide ,
annex , disband , or reorganize any organization of
the Active Militia , whenever in his judgment , the
cfficiency of the State Forces will be thereby in-
creased , and he shall have power to change the or-
ganization so as to conform to the regulations now
or hereafter prescribed by the laws of the United
States for the organization of the National Guard .
$ 10 . ACTIVE MILITIA , A DIVISION OF THE MILITARY
DEPARTMENT :
The Active Militia shall be a division of the Mili-
tary Department , and the Adjutant General shall ,
in addition to his other duties as Adjutant General ,
be the Executive Officer of this division , and shali
be responsible to the Governor for the proper func-
tioning of the Active Militia , and he is hereby fully
authorized and empowered to take the necessary
action to perfect and maintain an efficient organiza-
tion for the purposes herein set out . He shall have
charge of all matters of administration and organi-
zation, which shall be in all respects , insofar as
necessary and applicable , the same as that of the
National Guard .
11. EXECUTIVE COUNCIL :
For the purpose of assisting the Executive Of-
ficer in the organization and maintenance of the
Active Militia , an Executive Council of three ( 3 )
members is hereby created . Said Executive Coun-
cil shall consist of the three Senior Officers of the
Kentucky National Guard , who are hereby author-
ized and empowered to make and promulgate
through the Adjutant General, rules and regula-
tions for the organization and government of the
Active Militia ; to prescribe the uniforms and/or

No office in the Active Militia shall be incompa-

riots , mobs , great calamity , resistance to the proc-
esses of the State , or imminent danger of violence to
life or property and /or for the purpose of appre-
hending violators of any laws , including traffic or
motor vehicle laws , by written order to the Com-

such civil officer may deem necessary to assist the

lence to persons or property and to enforce any

any part of the Active Militia and the Commanding
Officer of the Militia ordered into service , shall , as
soon as possible , report the facts and circumstances
relating thereto to the Governor and the Governor
shall have the power to relieve from active duty any
Active Militiamen at any time he may deem proper .

CHAPTER 53 171
"Witnesses for the Commonwealth in felony
cases shall be allowed one dollar per day for each
day in attendance in the circuit court , after indict-
ments have been found , and beginning with the day
set for the trial of the case . Where witnesses reside
in another county, in addition to per diem, they
shall be allowed four cents ( .04 ) per mile going and
returning by nearest traveled route to be allowed
by the court and certified to the auditor for pay-
ment ; provided , said witnesses for the Common-
wealth as provided for in this section have been
summoned upon the written order of the county or
Commonwealth's attorney filed with the Clerk of the
court .
"Provided , however , that no witness shall be al-
lowed the per diem of one dollar per day for more
than one day unless said witness actually be used
on the trial or excused by the court or Common-
wealth's attorney on the first day said witness ap-
pears for said trial , and before such witness is sub-
poenead by the Commonwealth there has been filed
with the circuit clerk a statement signed by the Com-
monwealth's attorney or the county attorney to the
effect that such witness has been interviewed by said
county attorney or Commonwealth's attorney , and
that in the judgment of said county attorney or Com-
monwealth's attorney such witness is an important
one , and the clerk of said court shall not issue any
subpoena in Commonwealth's cases except upon the
written request of the county or Commonwealth's
attorney filed with the clerk of said court."
§ 2. All acts and parts of acts in conflict here-
with are hereby repealed .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

classification.title: CHAPTER 53 171
classification.summary: This statute outlines the per diem and mileage allowances for witnesses for the Commonwealth in felony cases in circuit court. It specifies that witnesses are allowed one dollar per day for attendance after indictment and beginning with the trial date. Witnesses from other counties receive four cents per mile. The statute also includes provisions for the payment of witnesses, requiring written orders from the county or Commonwealth's attorney and a statement that the witness has been interviewed and deemed important. It also states that no witness shall be allowed per diem for more than one day unless actually used on the trial or excused on the first day of appearance.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the compensation and procedural requirements for witnesses in felony cases. It does not contain any explicit or implicit racial classifications or restrictions. The provisions apply to all witnesses for the Commonwealth regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 54 .
AN ACT to amend and re -enact Section 346 of the Criminal Code
of Practice , Carroll's 1932 Edition .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That Section 346 of the Criminal Code of Prac-
tice , Carroll's 1932 Edition , be amended and re-
enacted so that when so amended and re-enacted it
shall read as follows :
" When a judgment of death has been affirmed , the
judgment of affirmance shall fix therein the day of
the execution as the fifth Friday following the date
of the mandate of the court . The Clerk of the Court
of Appeals shall transmit by special messenger or
by registered mail to the proper officer a certified
copy of said judgment and mandate , which shall be
the authority of such officer to carry said judgment
into effect . The officer receiving the same shall re-
port his action both to the governor and to the cir-
cuit court . If, from any cause , the execution does
not take place on the day appointed in said judg-
ment , the governor may from time to time appoint
another day for execution until the sentence is car-
ried into effect .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

classification.title: AN ACT to amend Section seven hundred seventy-two a of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes of nineteen hundred twenty-eight, relating to the discontinuance of passenger service by railroad.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous statute concerning railroad passenger service. It grants the Railroad Commission of Kentucky the power to authorize common carriers by railroad to discontinue passenger service on any line or branch if the service is operating at a loss with no reasonable probability of future improvement, or if the service has become unnecessary in the public interest. The amendment involves striking out the existing text of the section and inserting new provisions.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of railroad passenger service and the authority of the Railroad Commission to discontinue such services. It does not contain any explicit or implicit references to race, segregation, or differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'Act of the General Assembly' from 1934. The confidence is high because the text clearly focuses on economic and public interest factors for service discontinuation, with no racial component.

"After June 30 , 1920 , the manufacture , sale or
transportation of spirituous , vinous , malt or other
intoxicating liquors , except for sacramental , me-

Commonwealth of Kentucky , is hereby prohibited .
All sections or parts thereof of the Constitution , in-
sofar as they may be inconsistent with this section ,
are hereby repealed and nullified . The General

And by re-enacting Section 61 of the Constitution

"The general assembly shall , by general law , pro-

any county , city , town , district or precinct may be
taken , as to whether or not spirituous , vinous or
malt liquors shall be sold , bartered or loaned there-
in , or the sale thereof regulated . But nothing here-
in shall be construed to interfere with or to repeal
any law in force relating to the sale or gift of such
liquors . All elections on this question may be held
on a day other than the regular election days . '

On the first Monday in May , after the election of
these directors , representing the various drainage
districts in the County , they shall meet at the
County Seat, elect a President from one of their
number and appoint a secretary , who need not be a
director , and if no secretary is appointed , the
County Clerk of the County shall act as such and
be ex-officio secretary thereof . The President and
Directors shall receive no compensation for their
services and shall meet at such time or times as the
President may call them to meet , and the President
shall call a meeting when the request is made by
two or more directors which request shall be in
writing . The Board shall elect a treasurer , who
shall execute bond as the board directs and they
may pay said treasurer such salary as they deem
proper . Said commission shall have an office at the
County Seat of the County which shall be provided
with such furniture , stationery and records as may
be necessary .
The Board shall keep or cause to be kept com-
plete records of its proceedings , which records shall
be public records and at all times open to the in-
spection of the public . The Board of Drainage
Commissioners may employ a superintendent of
drainage if they deem such necessary , and for such
time as they deem necessary , and pay to him such
salary as they deem proper.

classification.summary: This chapter outlines the duties of the Superintendent of Public Instruction, including the printing and distribution of reports, the classification of schools, the taking and keeping of school census, and the approval of school building plans. It details the process for submitting rules and regulations for grading, classifying, and accrediting schools, as well as for determining curriculum scope and graduation requirements. It also describes the procedures for school census taking and the authority to conduct special censuses if accuracy is in doubt. Finally, it mandates the submission of plans and specifications for new public school buildings and alterations to the Superintendent.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details administrative and procedural aspects of the public education system in Kentucky, focusing on the responsibilities of the Superintendent of Public Instruction. It covers the reporting, classification, census-taking, and building plan approval processes. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' due to the chapter numbering and the context of legislative acts. The confidence is high because the content is clearly administrative and educational, with no racial implications.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Reenact Chapter 122 of the Kentucky Statutes Relating to Public Schools.
classification.summary: This act defines and reorganizes school districts in Kentucky. It specifies criteria for independent school districts, including those embracing cities of the first five classes and those with a school census enumeration of 250 or more white children. It also allows for temporary operation of independent districts with fewer than 250 white children under certain conditions, subject to the State Board of Education's approval.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'white children' in its criteria for defining and maintaining independent school districts. This explicit racial classification in educational policy is a hallmark of Jim Crow era legislation, which often segregated or differentiated educational resources and opportunities based on race. Therefore, it is classified as a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because of the explicit mention of race. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' from the citation 'GenAssemb 1934.pdf, page 288, year 1934', indicating it's an act passed by the General Assembly in that year.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Reenact Section 4399a-10 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to Independent School Districts
classification.summary: This statute outlines the operational requirements and conditions for temporary independent school districts in Kentucky. It mandates that independent school districts must provide an approved twelve-grade school service for white children and either establish or contract for an approved twelve-grade program for colored children. However, for independent districts in cities of the fifth or sixth class, the county board of education is responsible for providing educational facilities for colored children. The statute also details application procedures and conditions for operating as a temporary independent district.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'white children' and 'colored children' in the context of educational services, indicating a clear racial distinction in the provision of schooling. This differential treatment based on race is a hallmark of Jim Crow legislation. The statute falls under the 'education' category. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' from the source reference 'GenAssemb 1934.pdf, page 289, year 1934', which suggests it's an act passed by the General Assembly in 1934. The confidence is high due to the explicit racial language and the clear segregation in educational provisions.

classification.title: Merger of school districts
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for merging contiguous school districts in Kentucky. It details how boards of education can consolidate districts, how existing board members will serve out their terms, and how assets and liabilities will be transferred. It also specifies that tax levies for bond payments will continue to be collected over the original areas until all bonded obligations are retired. Additionally, it addresses the title to school property in transferred territory and allows boards to continue operating schools in other districts.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the administrative process of merging school districts. It does not contain any explicit or implicit racial classifications or restrictions. The language used is purely procedural and administrative, focusing on the logistics of district consolidation, asset transfer, and financial obligations. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 1934
classification.summary: This section of the 1934 Acts of the General Assembly outlines regulations for independent school districts and subdistricts within county school districts. It specifies conditions for establishing and maintaining schools, including limitations on purchasing sites or maintaining schools outside the county for districts in cities of the first or second class. Crucially, it states that no subdistrict shall exist with fewer than fifty white census pupils, though the State Board of Education can authorize emergency subdistricts with fewer pupils. It also details provisions regarding the modification of subdistrict boundaries, bonded indebtedness, and tax levies.
classification.reasoning: The statute is classified as Jim Crow because it implicitly discriminates based on race by setting a minimum requirement of 'fifty white census pupils' for a subdistrict to exist. This implies that non-white pupils were not counted towards this minimum, or that separate subdistricts for non-white students were not subject to the same minimum pupil requirements, or were simply not considered in the same administrative framework. This is a common tactic in Jim Crow legislation to maintain segregation and unequal educational resources. The confidence is 0.7 because while the racial implication is strong, it is not explicitly stated that non-white students were excluded from the count or that this was for segregation purposes, though historical context strongly suggests it. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 1934'.

A majority of the board shall constitute a quorum

board for reason satisfactory to it, shall be deemed

25. Vacancies ; how filled . Any vacancy in any
board of education , from whatever cause occurring ,
shall be filled for the unexpired term by the other
members of the board within 90 days after such
vacancy occurs , and in case the vacancy is not filled
by the other members of the board within said 90-
day period , it shall be filled by the State Board of
Education within 30 days after information has been
filed by any citizen of the district that such vacancy
has existed for more than 90 days . The member so
chosen shall hold office for the unexpired term and
until his successor is elected and qualified .
26. Exempt from jury duty and service as elec-
tion officer.-All board members shall be exempt
from jury duty and from service as election officer
during their term of office .

classification.summary: This statute outlines regulations for school superintendents and business directors within independent school districts in cities of the first and second classes. It prohibits superintendents from buying teachers' claims and from acting as an agent for textbook companies, with penalties for violations. It also details the appointment, responsibilities, and qualifications of a business director, who may be directly responsible to the board of education or serve as an assistant superintendent in charge of business affairs.

classification.title: An Act relating to school levies and budgets
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for school districts to make their annual school levies and present budgets. It specifies when the tax levying authority can make its annual school levy, the requirement to file a copy of the budget with the State Board of Education for approval, and the duty of the tax levying authority to levy the rate specified by the district board of education and approved by the State Board. It also details provisions regarding poll taxes, including a maximum amount and an exemption for independent districts embracing a city of the first class. The statute also addresses the consequences of failing to furnish a budget or make a levy within the prescribed dates and grants the tax levying authority for independent districts embracing a city of the first class the discretion to determine the rate of the school levy within set limits. Finally, it mandates that boards of education prepare and submit a close estimate or working budget to the State Board of Education under certain conditions.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the administrative and financial aspects of school districts, specifically concerning the process of levying taxes for school purposes and submitting budgets. While it mentions an exemption for poll taxes in independent districts embracing a city of the first class, this exemption is based on the classification of the city, not race. There are no explicit or implicit references to race or racial segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as a chapter from the Acts of the General Assembly of 1934.

the Commonwealth or through the execution of a

education of a district owning a fund for which it
is to receive only the income may, in its discretion ,
convert the same to its general school fund and
cause it to become a part of said fund ; provided
that there is no constitutional provision or other
lawful provision to the contrary . If two or more dis-
tricts are entitled to the income of such fund , then ,
upon concurrent action of the two boards , said fund
may be so converted and divided between them in
proportion to their census enumeration .

It details how members of existing systems are protected when a new system is established or an old one is reorganized, ensuring no loss of deposits. It also specifies conditions for members leaving the system before retirement and for the disposition of deposits and accrued interest in case of death. A key provision is the compulsory retirement age, which cannot exceed seventy years.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses the establishment and management of retirement systems, including provisions for member deposits, leaving the system, death benefits, and compulsory retirement age. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act relating to the establishment and maintenance of colleges for colored persons.
classification.summary: This act officially recognizes the establishment and maintenance of Kentucky State Industrial College in Frankfort and West Kentucky Industrial College in Paducah. It mandates that these two colleges for colored persons be supported by the Commonwealth with adequate resources to perform work comparable to other institutions of corresponding rank and kind. The purpose of these colleges is to train teachers for schools for colored children and to provide industrial training.

administration of the two colleges for colored per-
sons .
78. Meetings at institutions .- The State Board
of Education shall meet at least once each year at
the school plant of each of the colleges herewith
placed under its management and control , for the
purpose of inspecting the plant and observing the
work and the needs of the school .
79. Funds not to be diverted.-All appropria-
tions made by the General Assembly for the sup-
port of the said colleges for colored persons or for
the benefit thereof , and all grants , bequests , or
donations by any individual or corporation for spec-
ified use shall be applied to such use or uses and
no other .
80. Administration to be consistent with re-
mainder of act . In respects other than those spe-
cifically mentioned herein , the State Board of Edu-
cation , in its administration of the said colleges for
colored persons , shall be guided by existing legisla-
tion respecting its organization , duties , powers , and
activities.
Construction of Act
1. Construction of foregoing sections ; act sever-
able. The provisions of this act are severable , and
if any of its provisions shall be held unconstitu-
tional the decisions of the courts shall not affect
or impair any of the remaining provisions of this
act. It is hereby declared as a legislative intent
that this act would have been adopted had such un-
constitutional provisions not be included therein ,
and any school service prohibited or impaired by
such provisions shall be administered by the laws.
in effect at the time this act became operative .
Approved March 14 , 1934 .

364 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That counties whether organized under the
general law or special charter law are hereby author-
ized and empowered to establish and erect ele-
mentary and/or high school buildings , together with
necessary appurtenances thereto under the provi-
sions of this act for the purpose of supplying the
boards of education or boards of trustees of such
county or independent graded common school dis-
tricts located outside of the incorporated cities of
such county , adequate buildings necessary to carry
out their corporate duties and powers .
§ 2. For the purpose of defraying the cost of
acquiring any such elementary and/or high school
building by construction and appurtenances thereto ,
any such county may borrow money from the Public
Works Administration or other agency of the Fed-
eral Government and issue negotiable bonds , pro-
Ivided no such bonds shall be issued unless and until
authorized by a resolution specifying the proposed
undertaking, the amount of bonds to be issued and
the maximum rate of interest such bonds are to
bear , which shall not be more than six per cent
(6% ) per annum . Such resolution shall further pro-
vide that the proposed elementary and/or high
school buildings and appurtenances thereto are to
be constructed or acquired pursuant to the provi-
sions of this act .
3. All bonds under the provisions of this act
may be issued bearing interest at a rate not ex-
ceeding six per cent per annum , payable semi -an-
nually , and shall be executed in such manner and be
payable at such times , not exceeding thirty years
from the date thereof, and at such place or places
as such fiscal court or board of commissioners shall
determine .

WHEREAS , Many of the state educational institutions of Ken-
tucky are without proper and suitable dormitories , health
buildings and other physical facilities thus endangering the
health , safety , convenience and general welfare of the stu

WHEREAS , The existing condition of financial distress makes
it impossible for them to obtain such needed physical facili-
ties without supplementary financial assistance ; and ,
WHEREAS , The Federal Government through the Public Works
Administration of the National Industrial Recovery Act is of-
fering financial aid to the extent of a direct gift of 30 per cent
of the cost of labor and materials and a loan of the remain-
ing 70 per cent of the cost of labor and materials , at 4 per
cent interest , necessary for the erection of needed public
buildings for the use and benefit of society ; and ,
WHEREAS , There are no statutory provisions whereby boards
of regents and other governing bodies of such state educa-
tional institutions may qualify for financial assistance under
regulations for such grants and aids as is prescribed by the
Federal Emergency Administration of Public Works under the
National Industrial Recovery Act ; and ,

CHAPTER 84.
AN ACT governing the investments of funds of estates by guard-
ians and committees .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
Every guardian and committee shall invest the
funds of a ward's estate in such manner or in such
securities , in which the guardian or committee has
no interest , as allowed by law or approved by the
court or in annuities .
All laws or parts of laws in conflict herewith are
hereby repealed .
Whereas , any delay in the privilege of so invest-
ing the funds of a ward may result in a loss of the
principal or income to such ward , an emergency is
hereby declared to exist, and this Act shall take
effect upon its passage and approval by the Gov-
ernor .
Approved March 19, 1934 .

classification.title: AN ACT relating to the taxation of domestic life insurance companies
classification.summary: This act concerns the taxation of domestic life insurance companies. It establishes an annual state tax rate based on the fair cash value of the company's capital stock, designating it as an excise, license, or occupational tax for state purposes only. This tax is in lieu of all other taxes, except for real estate taxes and certain local taxes on companies maintaining their home office in a specific city, county, town, or district. The act also clarifies that the net cash value or reserve on outstanding policies is considered the property of policyholders and not subject to taxation by the insurance companies. It further amends and re-enacts Chapter 99 of the Acts of the General Assembly.

The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as a chapter from the Acts of the General Assembly of 1934.

classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning the taxation of domestic life insurance companies. It clarifies that the net cash value or reserve on outstanding policies is considered property of the policyholders and not subject to listing for taxation by the companies. Instead, domestic life insurance companies are required to pay an annual tax based on the fair cash value of their outstanding shares of capital stock. This tax is an excise, license, or occupational tax for state purposes only and is in lieu of other state, county, city, town, or other taxing district taxes, with the exception that the company must still list its real estate for taxation in each county where it is located.

classification.title: AN ACT amending and re-enacting Sections fourteen and sixteen of Chapter nineteen of the Acts of nineteen hundred sixteen, which are now Sections seven hundred sixty-two-a-sixteen, Carroll's Kentucky Statutes, relating to insurance agents; and repealing Section seven hundred sixty-two-a-fifteen, Carroll's nineteen hundred thirty Kentucky Statutes, which was Section fifteen of Chapter nineteen of the Acts of nineteen hundred and sixteen.
classification.summary: This act amends and re-enacts sections of a 1916 law concerning insurance agents. It redefines an insurance agent as an individual, partnership, or corporation that maintains an office in Kentucky, keeps complete records, does not sign blank policies for issuance outside their office, is a bona fide resident, and is authorized by an insurer qualified to do business in Kentucky. classification.title: AN ACT regulating insurance companies
classification.summary: This act amends existing statutes regarding insurance agents' licenses. It mandates that licenses expire annually on November 1st and require renewal applications from the company and payment of fees. A key provision states that no license will be renewed to an agent unless they are a bona fide resident of Kentucky. Furthermore, effective November 1, 1934, renewal requires the agent to file a statement conforming to a specific section of the Kentucky Statutes. The act also exempts assessment or co-operative associations, mutual companies, reciprocal or inter-insurance exchanges, and their representatives from its provisions. A separate section of the act, Chapter 92, deals with the investment of capital, surplus, and accumulations of insurance companies, defining necessary property for business operations, and providing penalties for violations. It also repeals a specific section of the Kentucky Statutes from 1930.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is an excerpt from the Acts of the General Assembly of Kentucky from 1934. The primary focus of the excerpt is the regulation of insurance companies and their agents, specifically concerning license renewal, residency requirements for agents, and investment practices of insurance companies. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The residency requirement applies to all agents seeking renewal, regardless of race. Therefore, this statute is not classified as a Jim Crow law.

Q. Investments Not Deemed Legal , When .
If the investments of any domestic insurance com-
pany hereafter made shall at the time of the mak-
ing thereof be otherwise than as authorized in this
act, such investments shall not be admitted or ac-
cepted as legal or authorized investments for such
company .
R. Prior Investments Not Affected .
Nothing herein shall be construed as affecting the
propriety or legality of an investment made by a do-
mestic insurance company which was , or is , in ac-
cordance wtih the laws in force prior to the passage
of this act or at the time of making thereof .
SECOND- Foreign Insurance Companies .
Any foreign or alien insurance company hereafter
licensed or relicensed to do business in this Com-
monwealth shall be possessed of assets of the same
general character as specified for domestic insur-
ance companies , except that investments authorized
by the law of the home state , state of United States
domicile , or country , of such company may be recog-
nized as legal investments , in the discretion of the
Insurance Commissioner .

CHAPTER 95 .
AN ACT to amend and re-enact Section 1093 of the Kentucky
Statutes (Carroll's 1930 Edition ) , being an Act defining and
prescribing the jurisdiction of justices and quarterly courts
and the judges of police courts in cities of the first , second ,
third , fourth , fifth and sixth class in this Commonwealth in
penal and misdemeanor cases .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That Section 1093 of the Kentucky Statutes (Car-
roll's 1930 Edition ) be repealed and re -enacted so
that same , when thus re-enacted shall read as fol-
lows :
" That justices , county and quarterly courts and
the judges thereof , and police courts in cities of
the first , second , third , fourth , fifth and sixth class
of this Commonwealth and the judges thereof , shall
have jurisdiction exclusive of circuit courts in ali
penal and misdemeanor cases , the punishment of
which is limited to a fine of not exceeding twenty
dollars , and jurisdiction concurrent with circuit
courts of all penal cases and misdemeanor cases ,
the punishment of which is limited to a fine of not
exceeding five hundred dollars , or imprisonment
not exceeding twelve months , or both ; but said
courts shall also have and exercise the same juris-
diction in the enforcement of city ordinances that
is now given to them by law, "

It details their roles in safety training, accident investigation, mine operations, and administrative tasks such as stationery and equipment provision. The text focuses on the operational and administrative aspects of mine safety and management.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the administrative and operational duties of the Department of Mines and Minerals and its inspectors in Kentucky. It focuses on mine safety, training, accident response, and general management of mining operations. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 1934'.

§ 10. Chief of the Department of Mines and
Minerals Shall Furnish Certified Copy of Inspection
Report. The Chief of the Department of Mines and
Minerals shall furnish to any person a certified copy
of the report of inspection of any mine inspected
by himself or by a district inspector, when ordered
to do so by any circuit court judge , which copy shall
be admissable in evidence in any court in this Com-
monwealth, and shall be prima facie evidence of the
truth of the recitals therein contained .
§ 11. Annual Report of the Department of Mines
and Minerals .- The Chief of the Department of
Mines and Minerals shall annually make report to
the Governor of the Commonwealth his proceedings
for and during the calendar year ending on the
thirty -first day of December . Said annual report
shall be prepared and printed within four months.
and as much earlier as possible after the close of the
calendar year . Said annual report shall list the
complete operation of the coal mines in this state ,
enumerating all accidents which have occurred in or
about the same , and giving such other information
as said Chief of the Department of Mines and Min-
erals may deem useful , and making such suggestions
as he may deem important as to further legislation
on the subject of mining .
§ 12. Operator's Monthly Report . For the pur-
pose of enabling the Chief of the Department of
Mines and Minerals to keep records and make re-

14. Mine Rescue Divisions .-The Chief of the
Department of Mines and Minerals is hereby author-
ized and directed to partition the coal fields of the
state into divisions which shall be known as mine
rescue divisions of which there shall be not less
than two in the western coal field and not less than
four in the eastern coal field . In each division there
shall be a station , the location of which shall be de-
termined by the Chief of the Department of
Mines and Minerals , at which station shall be kept
apparatus , appliances and supplies for use in the
work of rescue and relief upon the occurrence of ex-
plosions or mine fires , and for the training of of-
ficials and miners in such work .
The Chief of the Department of Mines and Min-
erals is hereby directed to provide each station , as

classification.title: An Act relating to mines and mining.
classification.summary: This act mandates monthly and annual reporting from mine owners, lessees, or superintendents to the Chief of the Department of Mines and Minerals. The reports must include information on accidents, number of persons employed, amount of coal mined, and other non-private data. It also directs the Chief to establish mine rescue divisions within the state's coal fields, with specific numbers of divisions for the western and eastern coal fields, and to equip stations within these divisions for rescue and relief work.

There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: CHAPTER 100 439
classification.summary: This statute outlines the appointment and qualifications of district mine inspectors and inspectors of mine weights. It details the extensive knowledge and experience required for these positions, including first aid, mine rescue, ventilation systems, handling of gases, blasting safety, mine fire management, electricity in mining, and mechanical aspects of mine scales. The Governor is responsible for appointing these inspectors, with a limit on the number of district inspectors and inspectors of mine weights.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the appointment, qualifications, and responsibilities of mine inspectors. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. The focus is solely on the technical and safety aspects of coal mining operations and the expertise required for inspectors. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

the Department of Mines and Minerals a report of
his proceedings during said calendar year and give
such information concerning the mines and mining
conditions in his district as may be appropriate and
required by the Chief of the Department of Mines
and Minerals . He shall visit each mine in his dis-
trict at least one ( once ) every three months , or
oftener if practicable to do so , and make a personal
examination of the interior of each mine with re-
spect to ventilation , drainage , timbering , blasting ,
electricity and general security as well as the out-
side of the mine , where any danger to the workmen
may exist , and he shall see that all provisions of this
act are strictly carried out .
$ 19. Ventilation of Mines .-Should the district
inspector discover any room, entry , airway or other
working places being driven in advance of the air
currents contrary to the requirements of this act , he
shall notify the mine foreman to cease work in such
places at once until the law is complied with .
The ventilation of all coal mines at which as many
as five men are employed shall be produced by means
of fans mechanically operated , located outside the
mine . Mines at which less than five men are em-
ployed shall be ventilated by means approved by
the Department of Mines and Minerals . Provided ,
however , that when inside fans are required to ven-
tilate working places on longwall or other modern
systems of mining , the Chief of the Department of
Mines and Minerals must first approve such in-
stallation .
In case of accident to the ventilating fan , or its
machinery whereby the ventilation of the mine
would be seriously interrupted , the mine foreman
shall order the men to withdraw immediately from
the mine and he shall not allow them to return to
their work until the ventilation has been restored

advance , break throughs for air shall be made sixty
feet apart , except where companies now are per-
mitted to drive with the approval of the District In-
spector and the Chief of the Department of Mines
and Minerals to ninety feet before turning break
throughs . Provided however , where longwall or
modern systems of mining are employed the Chief
of the Department of Mines and Minerals shall have
the authority to approve , at his discretion , a greater.
distance between break throughs or the method of
properly ventilating such longwall or modern sys-
tem of mining . All the break throughs between the
intake and return airways not required for the pas-
sage of air shall be closed with stoppings sub-
stantially built with suitable material , which shall be
approved by the district inspector, so as to keep
working places well ventilated.
As many as sixty persons shall be permitted to
work in the same air current , or split , provided how-
ever , that with the approval of the district inspector
and the Chief of the Department of Mines and
Minerals , as many as eighty persons may be per-
mitted to work in the same air current , or split .
No operator , superintendent or mine foreman
shall permit any persons to work where they are
unable to maintain a sufficient amount of ventilation ,
but this shall not be construed to prohibit the
operator from employing men to make the places of
employment safe to comply with this requirement ;
provided , further , that while the repair work neces-
sary to get the mine in condition to comply with the
law is in progress , no person or persons shall be per-
mitted to enter that part of the mine affected except
those actually employed in doing the necessary re-
pair work , said repair work to be done under the
constant supervision of an official designated by the
mine foreman .

classification.title: An Act to Amend Chapter 86 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to Mines and Mining
classification.summary: This statute outlines safety regulations for mines, specifically addressing ventilation requirements, the use of certain fuels for motive power, and the management of unused and abandoned mine sections to prevent gas accumulation. It mandates specific cubic feet of air per minute for miners and animals, requires substantial construction of stoppings and overcasts, and prohibits the use of petroleum or alcohol compounds that could contaminate the air. It also requires warning notices for abandoned sections.

§ 21. Shafts , Slopes and Drifts .-It shall not be
lawful for the operator, superintendent , or mine
foreman of any coal mine , whether worked by shaft ,
slope or drift , to permit persons to work therein un-
less there are to every seam of coal worked in such
mine at least two separate outlets , separated by a
natural strata of not less than one hundred feet in
breadth if the mine be worked by shaft or slope , and
separated by natural strata of not less than fifty
feet in breadth if the mine be worked by drift-by
which outlets distinct means of ingress and egress
are readily available to persons employed in such
mine ; but it shall not be necessary for the two out-
lets to belong to the same mine .
The foregoing requirements shall not apply to the
openings of a new entry of a mine that is being
worked for the purpose of making connection be-
tween said two outlets so long as not more than four
persons are employed at one time in making the con-
nection or driving the second opening ; nor shall said
requirements apply to any mine in which the second
opening has been rendered unavailable by reason of
final robbing or removal of pillars so long as not
more than twenty persons are employed therein at
one time.
In case any coal mine has but one shaft , slope , or
drift for ingress and egress of the persons employed
therein, and the owner thereof does not own suitable
ground for another opening , such owner may select
appropriate associate adjacent ground for that pur-
pose and have the same condemned and appropriate
the same by proceedings in the county court of the
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1934, p. 505

In every shaft used as an escape outlet there shall
be provided a stairway or ladderway of suitable
strength , design , and angle , which shall be provided
with platforms or landings at each turn of the stair-
way or ladderway , the whole to be subject to the ap-
proval of the district inspector and the Chief of the
Department of Mines and Minerals , or in lieu of
such stairway or ladderway there shall be provided
a properly equipped hoisting arrangement , consist-
ing of a cage suitable for hoisting men , a wire hoist-
ing rope of ample strength, and an adequate and
safe hoisting engine and drum, which hoisting ar-
rangement shall be ready for use at any time of
emergency . When the escape and fan shafts are
combined , the side or end used for an escape outlet
and the part used for ventilating purposes shall be
separated by a good , substantial air-tight partition
from top to bottom , so that the use of the escape
shaft shall not in any way interfere with the ventila-
tion of the mine . All shafts by which men enter or
leave the mine and the passageways to escape out-
lets shall be carefully examined by the mine fore-
man , or by a person designated by him , at least once
each week that the mine may be operating, and the
date and findings of such examination shall be
entered in a book kept at the mine for that purpose .
No debris , such as falls of slate and similar material ,
which shall act as an obstruction to the free passage
of men , shall be allowed to accumulate in the pas-
sageway to the escape outlet , nor shall bodies of
water difficult to pass be allowed to accumulate
there , nor shall the passageway be obstructed by
mine cars or timbers . If obstructions of any sort to
the free passage of men be found , such obstruction

ments shall be examined by the mine foreman or

§ 22. Checking Employees in and Out of Mine.-
The operator or Superintendent of every mine shall
install a uniform system of checking the employees
in and out of the mine , whereby each employee upon
entering the mine shall be given a check by which he
shall be identified , and which check shall be placed
in its proper place on the check board by the em-
ployee when leaving the mine .

The Chief of the Department of Mines and

at the mine the unsafe practices , defects , or un-

and proper condition for mining operations ; the
owner or operator of any mine so closed may apply
to the circuit court wherein such mine is located , or
the judge thereof in vacation , by petition for an
order directing said mine to be re -opened , and such
court , or the judge thereof in vacation , shall imme-diately hear and determine the matters arising upon
such petition , and if upon full hearing thereof the
court , or the judge thereof in vacation shall find that
said mine is in a reasonable safe condition the
prayer of said petition shall be granted ; but notice
of said hearing shall be given to the district mine
inspector and the Chief of the Department of Mines
and Minerals three days at the least before said
hearing ; and in all such hearings the Attorney Gen-
eral, the Commonwealth's Attorney , or the County
Attorney of the District or County in which the mine
is situated , shall appear for the state and defend thesame .

-In any mine or sections of mines liberating ex-
plosive gas where the lives of the persons employed
therein may be endangered , by the liberating of said
explosive gas the district inspector with the ap-
proval of the Chief of the Department of Mines and
Minerals may direct the operator , or superintendent
of any mine or mines to work said mine or mines ex-
clusively by the use of approved safety lamps and
approved electric lamps . The operator or super-
intendent of said mine or mines shall forthwith
carry out the recommendations of the district mine
inspector when so approved by the Chief of the De-
partment of Mines and Minerals .
In mines where flame safety lamps and approved
electric lamps are required to be used for working
therein , the lamps shall be in charge of some person
to be designated by the operator or superintendent ;
and at least two additional flame safety lamps shall
be kept at every coal mine whether such mine
liberates explosive gas or not. If deemed advisable
by the district inspector and the Chief of the De-
partment of Mines and Minerals all mine foremen
and fire bosses employed in gaseous mines shall , at
all times , carry an approved flame safety lamp for
the purpose of detecting the presence of explosive
gas , said lamp to be kept lighted at all times when in
use inside the mines .
In mines where approved safety lamps and ap-
proved electric lamps are used , no miner , workman ,
or other person shall at any time enter any mine or
carry therein any intoxicating liquors , matches ,
