in writing by the Chief of the Department of Mines

$ 30. Steam Locomotive .-No steam locomotive
shall be used in any mine in this state unless it be
approved in writing by the Chief of the Department
of Mines and Minerals .
§ 31. Explosives , Magazines , Where to be
Located . All distributing explosives magazines
used for quantities of explosives estimated as a
daily supply shall be located not less than one hun-
dred feet from any mine opening or building used
or occupied by any person or persons ; and the out-
side construction of such magazines shall be of non-
combustible material .
All magazines used for storing powder or other
explosives in greater quantities than an estimated
daily supply shall be located not less than five hun-
dred feet from any mine opening or building used or
occupied by any person or persons ; and the outside
construction of such magazines shall be of non-com-
bustible material , provided however , this shall not
apply to distributing or storage magazines already
constructed prior to the passage of this act .
Where Cardox or other explosives of a similar
nature are manufactured at or near the mine , the
Chief of the Department of Mines and Minerals shall
have the authority to prescribe the location and pro-
cedure of such installation .
§ 32. First Aid Training.-It shall be the duty
of each and every coal operator in this state to see
that all employees receive the necessary first aid
training for the safety of themselves and fellow-
workmen as recommended by the State Department
of Mines and Minerals . It shall be the duty of each
employee to receive the necessary training .

Any fire boss who neglects to comply with the
provisions of this Act relating to his duties , or who

Inspector for prosecution . If he be found guilty he
shall return his certificate of qualification as fire
boss to the Department of Mines and Minerals .
Nothing in this Article shall prevent a mine fore-
man holding a certificate of the first class from act-
ing as fire boss , or a regularly employed fire boss
from acting in an emergency as a mine foreman
holding such first class certificate .
§ 37. Shot Firers .-In any coal mine in this
state wherein the operator may choose to employ
practical and experienced persons as shot firers to
prepare the cartridge and set off and discharge the
shots he may do so .
Provided , however , in gaseous mines the Depart-
ment of Mines and Minerals may require the oper-
ator to employ practical and experienced persons as
shot firers and the Department of Mines and Min-
erals may prescribe the conditions under which
blasting may be carried on and the kind of explo-
sives that shall be used .

The mine foreman or his assistants shall visit and
carefully examine each working place in the mine
each day or oftener while the miners of such places
are at work and shall direct said miners so that each
and every working place shall be secured by props
or timbers where necessary . Should the mine fore-
man or his assistants find a place to be in a danger-
ous condition , they shall not leave the place until it

in until the place is made safe by some competent

The mine foreman shall see that every mine lib-
erating explosive gas is kept free of standing gas in
all working places and roadways . Any accumula-
tions of explosive gas or noxious gases in the worked
out or abandoned portions of any mine shall be re-
moved as soon as possible after its discovery . All
places in live sections that are temporarily aban-
doned shall be examined as live workings by the fire
boss on regular inspections . No person who may
be endangered by the presence of said explosive gas
or noxious gases shall be allowed in that portion
of the mine until said gases have been removed . The
mine foreman shall direct and see that all dangerous
places and the entrance or entrances to worked out
and abandoned places in all mines are properly
fenced off across the openings , so that no person
can enter , and that danger signals are posted upon
said fencing to warn persons of existing danger .
Any mine in which explosive gas can be detected
with a flame safety lamp shall require the services
of a mine foreman of the first class . Any mine in
which explosive gas is not present may employ the
services of a mine foreman of the second class .

CHAPTER 100 467
The mine foreman shall give prompt attention to
the removal of all dangers reported to him by his
assistants , the fire boss , or any other person work-
ing in the mine , and in case it is impracticable to
remove the danger at once, he shall notify every per-
son whose safety is menaced thereby to remain
away from the portion where the dangerous condi-
tion exists . He or his assistants shall , at least once
every week , travel and examine all the air courses ,
roads and openings that give access to old workings
or falls , and make a record of the condition of all
places where danger has been found , with ink , or
indelible pencil , in the record book provided for that
purpose .
The mine foreman shall notify , in writing , the
operator or superintendent of the mine of his in-
ability to comply with any of the requiremnets of
these sections , and it shall then become the duty of
any operator , or superintendent , to at once attend
to the matter complained of by the mine foreman
so as to enable him to comply with the provisions
thereof .
The mine foreman shall each day enter and sign
plainly with ink or indelible pencil , in a book pro-
vided for that purpose , a report of the condition of
the mine, which report shall clearly state any danger
that may come under his observation during the
day , or any danger reported to him by his assistants
or the fire bosses . The report shall also state
whether or not there is a proper supply of material
on hand for the safe working of the mine , and
whether or not the requirements of the law are com-
plied with.
The mine foreman shall, also each day , read care-
fully , and countersign with ink or indelible pencil ,
all reports entered into the record book of the fire
bosses .

classification.title: CHAPTER 100 .481
classification.summary: This statute outlines the role and responsibilities of a "Check Weighman" in coal mines. It details their appointment, access to mine scales, limitations on other duties, and the requirement that they not hinder the operator's weighman or employees. It also specifies that the operator must ensure cars are tared at reasonable intervals and that if a suitable employee is not available, an external person can be selected by mutual agreement. Additionally, it grants mine operators the right to adopt special rules for mine operation, subject to approval by the Chief of the Department of Mines and Minerals.
classification.reasoning: The statute discusses regulations for coal mines, specifically concerning the role of a 'Check Weighman' and mine rules. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The focus is purely on labor and operational regulations within the mining industry. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 1 of Chapter 103 of the Acts of the General Assembly of 1930.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 1 of Chapter 103 of the Acts of 1930. It exempts cities of the fourth, fifth, and sixth classes from certain requirements (related to selling, conveying, leasing, mortgaging, or encumbering water works or lighting systems) when they issue revenue bonds under specific 1926 and 1930 acts for the purpose of acquiring or improving such systems. The amendment allows these cities to issue revenue bonds without the previously mandated election notice period.

CHAPTER 114 .
AN ACT providing for the payment of ad valorem property
taxes of fourth class cities , in two equal installments , due as
follows : July first and December first ; providing that the
second installment shall be deemed due and payable as of
the same day as the first installment in case the first install-
ment is not paid within thirty days after its due date ; pro-
viding that all taxes shall be deemed delinquent if not paid
on or before the first day of December following their due
dates ; prescribing penalties for delinquency; providing for
part payment of taxes before or after their due dates and dis-
count on taxes paid before they become due .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. The city Council , Commissioners or other
governing bodies of fourth class cities , may pre-
scribe , by ordinance , duly enacted , on or before
July first of any year , as follows , to -wit : All annual
city taxes on all property subject to general levies
for annual ad valorem taxation shall be due and
payable in two equal installments . The first one-
half of such taxes shall be due and payable on the
first day of July after the assessment ; the second
one-half on the first day of December next succeed-
ing ; provided that , if the first such installment of
such taxes is not paid on or before the first day of
August , both installments shall be deemed to have
become due and payable on the said first day of July .

CHAPTER 116 .
AN ACT for the benefit of Mrs. Alice Moneymaker , widow of
W. L. Moneymaker , who lost his life in the service of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky .
WHEREAS , W. L. Moneymaker was a guard at the State peni-
tentiary at Eddyville , and while on duty was brutally assaulted
and fatally injured by a convict ; and ,
•WHEREAS , the said W. L. Moneymaker was receiving , at the
time of his death , a salary of one hundred ( 100 ) dollars per
month for his services as guard ; and ,
WHEREAS , he left a widow, Mrs. Alice Moneymaker , and two
minor children , namely , Woodrow Moneymaker and Mary
Belle Moneymaker ; and ,
WHEREAS , the said Woodrow Moneymaker , who has since
died, was a helpless invalid , and the said Mary Belle Money-
maker , now of age , was and is a semi -invalid ; and ,
WHEREAS , the family of the said W. L. Moneymaker was left
in indigent circumstances ; now , therefore,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That the Auditor of Public Accounts is di-
rected to draw his warrant upon the State Treas-
urer for the sum of One Thousand ( 1,000 ) Dollars
in favor of the said Mrs. Alice Moneymaker.
$ 2. That this Act shall take effect from and
after its passage .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

CHAPTER 129.
AN ACT providing for the consolidation of the office of jailer
with that of sheriff in each county of the State .
§ 1. The office of jailer is hereby consolidated
with that of sheriff , in each county of the state ,
under the provisions of Section 105 of the Constitu-
tion . There are hereby transferred to and vested in
the sheriff , all the powers and duties heretofore au-
thorized by law to be exercised or performed by the
jailer . Wherever in any law of the State, reference
is made to the jailer , such reference shall be deemed
to apply to the sheriff , except where the context re-
quires otherwise .
§ 2. All laws and parts of laws in conflict with
this act , are hereby repealed to the extent of such
conflict .
3. This act shall take effect at the expiration
of the term of the jailers in office when the act is
passed .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

ized from any sale authorized herein shall be ap-

annum , which interest shall be paid as same falls
due , by the Commonwealth of Kentucky .
The President and Secretary of the State Board
of Health shall draw their warrants for said interest
in installments as they fall due upon the Auditor
of Public Accounts , payable to the Secretary of said
Board , and upon receipt of said draft by the
Auditor , he shall draw his warrants for the proper
amount upon the Treasurer of the State . The money
authorized to be paid out of the State Treasury by
this Act shall be paid out of the General Fund .
2. Inasmuch as the construction of this build-
ing is a part of the National Recovery Program and
is to provide for the immediate employment of labor
and in the improvement of the property of the
State of Kentucky an emergency is declared to exist
and this Act shall take effect and be in force after
its approval by the Governor .

ized to possess , sell and dispense said drugs , who

any person said drug , or otherwise deal in the same

or any person who shall forge or utter a forged pre-
scription for the same , shall thereby render him-
self amenable to the penalties as herein provided .

§ 4. ( Qualification for Licenses . ) No license
shall be issued under the foregoing section unless
and until the applicant therefor has furnished proof
satisfactory to the State Board of Health .
( a) That the applicant is of good moral char-
acter or, if the applicant be an association or cor-
poration , that the managing officers are of good
moral character .
(b ) That the applicant is equipped as to land,
buildings , and paraphernalia properly to carry on
the business described in his application .
No license shall be granted to any person who
has within five years been convicted of a willful
violation of any law of the United States , or of any
state , relating to opium , coca leaves , or other nar-
cotic drugs , or to any person who is a narcotic drug
addict.
The State Board of Health may suspend or re-
voke any license for cause .
§ 5. ( Sale on written orders . )
(1 ) A duly licensed manufacturer or wholesaler
may sell and dispense narcotic drugs to any of the
following persons , but only on official written or-
ders :
(a) To a manufacturer , wholesaler , or apothe-
cary .
(b) To a physician , dentist , or veterinarian .
(c) To a person in charge of a hospital, but only
for use by or in that hospital .
( d) To a person in charge of a laboratory , but
only for use in that laboratory for scientific and
medical purposes.

drugs in a pharmacy upon discontinuance of dealing
in said drugs , may sell said stock to a manufacturer ,

written order .
( 3 ) An apothecary , only upon an official written
order , may sell to a physician , dentist, or veterina-
rian , in quantities not exceeding one ounce at any
one time , aqueous or oleaginous solutions of which
the contest of narcotic drugs does not exceed a pro-
portion greater than twenty percent of the complete
solution , to be used for medical purposes .
§ 7. (Professional use of Narcotic Drugs . )
( 1 ) (Physicians and Dentists . ) A physician or
a dentist , in good faith and in the course of his
professional practice only, may prescribe , admin-
ister , and dispense narcotic drugs , or he may cause
the same to be administered by a nurse or interne
under his direction and supervision .
(2) (Veterinarians . ) A veterinarian , in good
faith and in the course of his professional practice
only, and not for use by a human being , may pre-
scribe , administer , and dispense narcotic drugs , and
he may cause them to be administered by an assist-
ant or orderly under his direction and supervision .
(3) (Return of unusued drugs . ) Any person
who has obtained from a physician , dentist , or vet-
erinarian any narcotic drug for administration to
a patient during the absence of such physicians ,
dentist , or veterinarian , shall return to such physi-
cian , dentist , or veterinarian any unused portion of
such drug , when it is no longer required by the pa-
tient.

§ 13. (Common Nuisances . ) Any store , shop ,
warehouse , dwelling house , building , vehicle , boat ,
aircraft, or any place whatever , which is resorted to
by narcotic drug addicts for the purpose of using
narcotic drugs or which is used for the illegal keep-
ing or selling of the same , shall be deemed a com-
mon nuisance. No person shall keep or maintain
such a common nuisance .
§ 14. (Narcotic Drugs to be delivered to State
Officials , Etc. ) All narcotic drugs , the lawful pos-
session of which is not established or the title to
which cannot be ascertained , which have come into
the custody of a peace officer , shall be forfeited .
and disposed of as follows :
(a) Except as in this section otherwise provid-
ed , the court or magistrate having jurisdiction shall
order such narcotic drugs forfeited and destroyed .
A record of the place where said drugs were seized ,
of the kinds and quantities of drugs so destroyed ,
and of the time , place , and manner of destruction ,
shall be kept , and a return under oath , reporting
said destruction , shall be made to the court or magis-
trate and to the United States Commissioner of
Narcotics , by the officer who destroys them .
(b) Upon written application by the State Board
of Health , the court or magistrate by whom the for-
feiture of narcotic drugs has been decreed may order
the delivery of any of them, except heroin and its
salts and derivatives , to said State Board of Health
for distribution or destruction , as hereinafter pro-
vided .

§ 15. ( Notice of Conviction to Be Sent to Li-
censing Board . ) On the conviction of any person

of the judgment and sentence , and of the opinion

shall be sent by the clerk of the court , or by the

court may , in its discretion , suspend or revoke the
license or registration of the convicted defendant
to practice his profession or to carry on his business .
On the application of any person whose license or
registration has been suspended or revoked, and
upon proper showing and for good cause , said board
or officer may reinstate such license or registration .

§ 16. (Records , Confidential . ) Prescriptions ,
orders , and records , required by this act , and stocks

§ 17. (Fraud or Deceit . )
(1 ) No person shall obtain or attempt to obtain
a narcotic drug , or procure or attempt to procure
the administration of a narcotic drug, ( a) by fraud ,
deceit , misrepresentation , or subterfuge ; or ( b ) by
the forgery or alteration of a prescription or of any
written order ; or ( c) by the concealment of a ma-
terial fact ; or (d ) by the use of a false name or the
giving of a false address .
(2) Information communicated to a physician
in an effort unlawfully to procure a narcotic drug ,
or unlawfully to procure the administration of any
such drug , shall not be deemed a privileged com-
munication .
(3 ) No person shall willfully make a false state-
ment in any prescription , order , report , or record ,
required by this act.
(4 ) No person shall , for the purpose of obtain-
ing a narcotic drug , falsely assume the title of , or
represent himself to be a manufacturer , wholesaler,
apothecary , physician , dentist , veterinarian , or
other authorized person .
(5) No person shall make or utter any false or
forged prescription or false or forged written order .

classification.title: CHAPTER 145
classification.summary: This statute outlines the duties, eligibility, and removal procedures for commissioners. It specifies that commissioners must devote their entire time to their duties, cannot be candidates for public office or join political party committees (vacating their office if they do), and are ineligible for election to public office for two years after ceasing to be a commissioner. The Governor can remove a commissioner for cause after providing charges and an opportunity for a public hearing. A majority of commissioners constitutes a quorum, and the act of a majority is the act of the commission. Investigations can be delegated to designated commissioners.

classification.title: ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 1934

The order must specify how the proceeds will be used. The commission can grant, deny, or modify applications.

$ 9. Penalties .
Every officer , agent or employee of any utility as
enumerated in section 1 hereof, or other person who
shall willfully violate any provision of this act , or
who procures , aids or abets any violation of this act
by any such utility shall be guilty of a misdemeanor
and upon conviction thereof shall be fined not more
than one thousand ( $ 1,000.00 ) dollars , or be confined
in jail not more than six ( 6 ) months , or both ; and if
any such utility shall be a private corporation and
shall violate any of the provisions of this act , or
shall do any act herein prohibited , or shall fail and
refuse to perform any duty imposed upon it under
this act for which no penalty has been provided by
law , or who shall fail , neglect or refuse to obey any
lawful requirement or order made by the commis-
sion , for every such violation , failure or refusal such
utility shall forfeit and pay into the treasury a sum
not less than twenty-five ($ 25.00 ) dollars , nor more
than one thousand ( $ 1,000.00 ) dollars , for each such
offense , said sum or sums to be paid to the Treas-
urer and credited to the general fund . In constru-
ing and enforcing the provisions of this section the
act , omission or failure of any officer , agent or other
person acting for or employed by any utility acting
within the scope of his employment shall in every
case be deemed to be the act , omission or failure of
such utility.
Actions to recover the principal amount due and
penalties under this Act shall be brought in the
name of the Commonwealth of Kentucky in the
Franklin Circuit Court . Whenever any utility is
subject to a penalty under this Act , the commission
shall certify the facts to the Commission Counsel
who shall institute and prosecute an action for re-
covery of such principal amount due and the pen-

poses of medicinal use as provided in this Act such
liquors have medicinal value and the constitution is

Act constitutes the most effective appropriate legis-
lation under the condition now confronting the
Commonwealth , and this is enacted in order to har-
monize the law , prevent lawlessness , protect the in-
nocent , punish the guilty , and provide its lawful
and just revenue and taxation for the Common-

classification.title: An Act relating to local option elections and the sale of liquor.

If a majority votes to prohibit liquor sales, the prohibition takes effect 60 days after the canvassing board's certificate is entered. Licenses become void, and license holders can seek proportional refunds. Penalties are established for selling liquor after the prohibition takes effect, including fines and jail time. It also penalizes those who knowingly furnish or rent property for illegal liquor sales, making the property liable for fines.

classification.title: AN ACT amending Section 907a, Kentucky Statutes, Carroll's 1930 Edition, to change the uniform fiscal year for counties in the state to end on June 30 instead of December 31, of each calendar year.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 907a of the Kentucky Statutes to change the uniform fiscal year for counties from ending on December 31st to ending on June 30th of each calendar year. It also establishes a new system for annual occupational licenses, requiring them to be obtained and paid by June 30th annually. Licenses expiring after June 30, 1934, will be prorated until the next June 30th. If a license is obtained for part of a year, the fee will be prorated based on the unexpired months.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The changes are administrative and financial in nature. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

WHEREAS , many of said boards , officers , commissions and
agencies have the same or overlapping functions , duties and
powers , involving a needless expenditure of public funds ; and
WHEREAS , by merging some , abolishing others and creating
certain others by a combination of two or more existing agen-
cies , or boards , a more efficient and economical governmental
service can be rendered the State , now, therefore ,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
ARTICLE I- SHORT TITLE AND DEFINITIONS
§ 1. Short Title . This Act shall be known as
the " Administrative Reorganization Act ."
§ 2. Definitions of Terms . In this Act, except
where the context requires otherwise , the words and
expressions defined in this section shall be held to
have the meaning here given to them :
" Administrative Department " means any agency
of the State named in this Act as an administrative
department .
"Agency" means any State officer , department ,
board , commission , institution , division , or other
person or functional group heretofore existing or
hereby or hereafter created , that is authorized to
exercise , or that does exercise any function of gov-
ernment of the State .
" Constitution " means the Constitution of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky .

It then transitions to the organization of state departments.

classification.title: CHAPTER 155
classification.summary: This statute outlines the organization and functions of the Department of Public Property in Kentucky. It details the attachment of commissions to the department, the employment of clerical staff, the appointment and removal of a Superintendent of Public Buildings, and the appointment of other superintendents and commissioners. The department is given control over all state property, real or personal, and requires written approval for any state purchases. It also mandates that no printing of any kind or class, or any paper for the use of the state, can be done without prior approval.

It focuses on the management of state assets, procurement, and printing services. ARTICLE XXIII —THE EXECUTIVE CABINET
The Governor , Secretary of State , Auditor , At-
torney General , Chairman of the State Highway
Commission , Superintendent of Public Instruction ,
Commissioner of Agriculture , Labor and Statistics ,
Treasurer , Chairman of the State Tax Commission ,
Adjutant General , Chairman of the Department of
Public Welfare , Commissioner of Insurance and the
Commissioner of Banking and Securities shall con-
stitute the Governor's Executive Cabinet . They
shall meet at least once monthly at such times and
places as may be provided by rule , and at other
times and places upon the call of the Governor .
The Executive Cabinet shall consider such mat-
ters involving general administrative policies and
procedure , relations between departments and other
agencies and desirable measures for cooperation be-
tween departments and other agencies as the Gov-
ernor , the Secretary of the Executive Cabinet , or
any member may place before it , and shall advise
and consult with the Governor at his request upon
all matters affecting the welfare of the State and
relating to the several departments and agencies .
The Governor shall be Chairman of the Executive
Cabinet. The Executive Cabinet and the Secretary
thereof, shall be deemed to be attached to the office
of the Governor and not constitute a separate de-
partment or agency of the State .

CHAPTER 155 709
There shall be a Secretary of the Cabinet who
shall be appointed by the Governor upon the basis
of his qualifications therefor . The qualifications for
such appointment shall include four (4 ) years of
responsible experience in the management of sub-
stantial public or private business enterprises . The
Secretary of the Executive Cabinet shall prepare all
matters for consideration by the Executive Cabinet .
The Secretary of the said Cabinet shall also be the
Secretary of the Department of Finance and
Budgetary Control and of the Department of Public
Property. His salary shall be fixed by the Governor
within the constitutional limits , and he shall hold
office at the pleasure of the Governor . He shall keep
the minutes and records of the Executive Cabinet .
He shall make continuous studies and accumulate
and compile information for the use of the Execu-
tive Cabinet and the members thereof and the Gov-
ernor relative to the business management and the
operation of the State government , and shall cur
rently supply the members with copies of available
reports and documents and other material relating
to the conditions and problems of the State , the dif-
ficulties that individual departments and agencies
have to overcome , the manner in which the problems
of each department and other agencies relate to
those of other departments and of other agencies ,
and to the State government , and the methods by
which similar problems have been met and solved in
other jurisdictions . He shall assist the Governor in
handling and in securing a coordination of the ef-
forts of the several departments and other agencies
with respect to matters of administration which do
not pertain specifically to individual departments or
other agencies .
The Secretary of the Executive Cabinet shall not
hold office in the organization of any political party .

CHAPTER 155 711
ARTICLE XXIV- GENERAL PROVISIONS
§ 1. Transfer of Functions . Whenever functions
heretofore vested in , or exercised by, any agency
are by this Act transferred in whole or in part to
and vested in a department or other agency created
or continued by this Act, the department or other
agency in which such functions are vested shall be
the successor to the agency in which such functions
have heretofore been vested and every act done in
the exercise of such functions by or under the
authority of the department or other agency to
which such functions are hereby transferred , shall
have the same force and effect as if done by the
former agency . Whenever such former agency is
referred to , or designated in any law , contract or
document , such reference or designation shall be
deemed to refer to the department or other agency ,
hereunder to which the functions of such former
agency so referred to or designated have been trans-
ferred and vested by the terms of this Act .
§ 2. Transfer of Records and Equipment . Upon
the taking effect of any transfer of functions pro-
vided by this Act , all books , papers , records , prop-
erty and pending business theretofore made , used ,
acquired or conducted by any previously existing
agency in the exercise of functions are hereby trans-
ferred to and vested in the agency succeeding to
such functions .
§ 3. Transfer of Officers and Employees . All
officers and employees whose functions are trans-
ferred hereby from an existing agency and vested
in a different agency , created or continued by the
terms of this Act, shall be transferred with their
functions to the department in which their functions
or the major part thereof are hereby vested , and
shall so far as practicable continue to perform their
usual duties until the expiration of their respective

§ 9. When the Act Is Effective . The provisions
of this Act shall be and become effective at 12 o'clock
midnight , June 30 , 1934 .
$ 10 . Inconsistent Laws Repealed . It is the pur-
pose of this Act to provide a uniform , integrated
and comprehensive plan of organization for the
executive departments of the Government of the
Commonwealth for the exercise of all executive and
administrative functions of the State . All laws and
parts of laws in conflict herewith are hereby repealed
to the extent of such conflict .

classification.summary: This act amends a previous act concerning cities of the second class. It specifically modifies Section 6, which deals with the purchase of bridges. The acquisition is to be on terms and prices deemed reasonable by the commission, with title taken in the city's name, subject to any existing mortgages or liens.

classification.title: An Act relating to the qualification of bidders on contracts with the State Highway Commission.
classification.summary: This act outlines the requirements for bidders on contracts with the State Highway Commission. It specifies that applications for qualification must be general and not indicate contract size. Foreign corporations must provide proof of authorization to do business in their state of incorporation. The act emphasizes that its provisions and rules apply equally to residents and non-residents of Kentucky. The Commission can require bidders to name subcontractors and will not consider bids from unqualified persons, firms, associations, or corporations. Bids from unqualified bidders discovered before reading will be returned, and if disqualifying facts are found after opening, the bid will be rejected. Awarding a contract to an unqualified bidder is unlawful. The act does not apply to direct purchases of equipment, materials, and supplies, except for crushed stone in lots over one thousand.

classification.title: AN ACT to prevent the destruction or marring of trees, shrubs and other flora growing or being on the rights of way of State or County maintained roads.
classification.summary: This act prohibits the picking, pulling, tearing, digging, cutting, breaking, injuring, burning, or destroying of trees, shrubs, wildflowers, flowers, vines, bushes, or turf growing on the right-of-way of state or county highways, or on public parks, refuges, or sanctuaries. It also prohibits depositing trash, debris, or litter on these areas without written permission from the relevant authority. Violators are guilty of a misdemeanor and subject to a fine of not less than one dollar nor more than twenty-five dollars.

any toll bridge or ferry without paying the required toll ; mak-

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :

bridge or ferry , upon which bridge or ferry tolls are
at such time collected , without paying to the col-
lector of tolls then stationed at such bridge or ferry
at the time of such passage , in legal tender , such

lation for passage over such bridge or ferry , includ-
ing vehicular traffic over or upon any part of such
bridge or ferry ; provided that no such toll shall be
charged to children going to or returning from
school . Neither shall such toll be charged for school
busses or other vehicles used exclusively in the
transportation of school children to and from school .
2. That it shall be unlawful for any person or
persons to park any vehicle upon any toll bridge at
any time.
3. Any person or persons found guilty of
violating any of the provisions of this act shall be
guilty of a misdemeanor and upon conviction thereof
shall be fined not less than $10.00 nor more than
$100.00 for each offense .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

The project begins at Whippowill bridge on Highway No. 68 and extends via a practical route to Highway No. 15 near Lewisburg, known as the Rush Mill Road. It also establishes another project beginning near Cave Spring on the Clarksville highway and extending to the intersection of Orndorff Mill road at Dot, known as the Watermelon Road. Both projects are to be entitled to all benefits and subject to all laws related to the primary system of roads and highways.

AN ACT relating to establishing as a part of the primary sys-

Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That there is hereby established as a part of the
primary system of the State Highways of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky , a road running from a
point near the south corporate limits of the town of
Crittenden in Grant County, Kentucky , on U. S. 25 ;

State Highways .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

CHAPTER 288 .
AN ACT to establish and make a road a part of the primary
road system of the State of Kentucky , leading from Morning
View , Kenton County , Kentucky , west of the L. & N. Rail-
road right -of-way , south through the farms of Dunn , Dudgeon ,
Hagerdon , Tomlin , Al Mullins and Nat Mullins to Demossville ,
Pendleton County , Kentucky .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That there is hereby created and established
as a part of the primary road system of the State
of Kentucky a road leading from Morning View,
Kenton County , Kentucky , west of the right -of-way
of the Louisville & Nashville Railroad Company ,
south through the farms of Dunn , Dudgeon , Hager-
don, Tomlin , Al Mullins and Nat Mullins to Demoss-
ville , Pendleton County , Kentucky .
§ 2. Said road , when established , to be construct-
ed, maintained , supervised and controlled in the
same way, manner , terms and conditions as set out
and provided for in Chapter 17 , page 76 , of the Acts
of the General Assembly at its 1920 session estab-
lishing and creating a primary system of State
roads , and same to be designated as Project No.............
§ 3. All acts or parts of acts in conflict herewith
are hereby repealed .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

classification.title: AN ACT relating to roads and highways and describing and establishing a project to be added to the state primary system of roads
classification.summary: This act establishes two road projects to be added to the state primary system of highways in Kentucky. The first project runs from Fred Mayfield on the Williamsburg road west to Corbin-Cumberland Falls road near Corinth and will be known as the Will Smith Highway. The second project runs from near Woffard, eastward up Browns Creek, down Maple Creek to Fred Kerr on the Williamsburg-Pineville road and will be known as the R. D. Sullivan pike. Both projects are to receive all benefits of laws relating to the primary system of highways.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the establishment of new road projects to be added to the state's primary highway system. It names the roads and specifies their routes. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

tem of highways of the Commonwealth of Kentucky a road ,
known as the Old Rush Mill Road , beginning at a point in

mately twelve miles , intersecting the Lewisburg - Claymore

That there be and there now is established as

poorwill Bridge at ten miles west of Russellville in

ernly direction crossing the Highland Lick Road
about ten miles west of Russellville in Logan county
and running in a northernly direction via village of

CHAPTER 299 831
CHAPTER 299 .
AN ACT to add to the Primary System of public roads the
road leading from State Project No. 2, known as U. S. High-
way No. 41 , leading from Trenton to Pinchem , and inter-
secting and crossing Highway No. 98 at that point, and con-
tinuing in an easterly direction to Old Haydensville , inter-
secting and crossing U. S. Highway No. 81 , and continuing in
a southerly direction to a point about one mile south of New
Haydensville , thence in an easterly direction , passing through
Graham's Mill to the Logan County line.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That Section 4356T -5 of Article 2 , Chapter 110 of
the Kentucky Statutes , 1922 Edition , relating to
public roads , be amended by adding and inserting
the following Project , No. extending from
Project No. 2 , known as U. S. Highway No. 41-E , at
Trenton in Todd county , running in an easterly
direction to Pinchem , intersecting and crossing
Highway No. 98 at that point , and continuing in an
easterly direction to Old Haydensville , intersecting
and crossing U. S. Highway 81 at that point , con-
tinuing in a southerly direction , passing through
New Haydensville to a point about one mile south
of New Haydensville , thence in an easterly direction
passing through Graham's Mill to the Logan county
line , being known as the old Trenton and Gallatin
road .
—
This route when established to be designed as
Project No. and to be entitled to and receive all
the benefits relating to the Primary System of roads
and highways . The State Highway Commission
will have said route surveyed and located .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

CHAPTER 336 .
AN ACT creating and establishing as part of the primary sys-
tem of highways of the Commonwealth of Kentucky a road
beginning at or near Lily , Laurel County, on State Highway
No. 25 and running to the intersection of the proposed Lon-
don -Barbourville Route No. 144 at or near Camp Ground,
Laurel County, Kentucky .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That there be and there now is established as a
part of the primary system of highways of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky a road beginning at or
near Lily, Laurel County , on State Highway No.
25 and running to the intersection of the proposed
London - Barbourville Route No. 144 at or near
Camp Ground , Laurel County , Kentucky.
The road now established as a part of the primary
system of highways of the Commonwealth of Ken-
tucky shall be given an appropriate number by the
Kentucky State Highway Commission , and shall be
entitled to all the rights , privileges , benefits and
conditions of all the laws now in force or which may
hereafter be enacted affecting the primary road
system .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

system of the Commonwealth of Kentucky : A road beginning

Lewisport (Johnson's Park ) ; thence via Jewell Mill Dam to
Blackford Ditch ; then following the dump of said Blackford
Ditch to Patesville , thence via Glendean to a point connect-
ing with the Axtel road ; and then following roads now ou
primary system to Hardin Springs in Hardin County , and
combining these roads making them one road with an ap-
propriate number .

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the

privileges , benefits and conditions of all of the Pri-
mary System that is now and may hereafter be
created .

The first road runs from Elizaville through Nepton to Route 68 in Mason County. The second road starts at Hillsboro in Fleming County, goes through Sunset, follows a dirt road to Days Mill pike, and then continues to Tilton, intersecting Fields Highway.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text describes the establishment of new roads as part of Kentucky's primary road system. It details the routes and counties involved. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest a discriminatory purpose or effect. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly in 1934. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about infrastructure and lacks any racial element. Human review is not needed.

CHAPTER 421 .
AN ACT relating to roads and bridges and establishing as a
part of the primary system of State highways a road lead-
ing from United States Highway No. 227 at Wheatley in Owen
County, Kentucky ; thence in an easterly direction by Souther
Brother's farm to the old New Liberty and Moxley pike at
old Toll Gate House on Little Twin Creek ; thence by J. J.
McNeal and L. P. Alexander farms to Moxley , near the Ken-
tucky River on the Owen County side thereof , and designating
the same as State Project No.-
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That there is hereby established as a part
of the Primary System of State Highways of Ken-
tucky a road beginning at and leading from United
States Highway No. 227 at Wheatley in Owen coun-
ty, Kentucky ; thence in an Easterly direction by
Souther Brother's farm to the old New Liberty and
Moxley pike at old Toll Gate House on Little Twin
Creek ; thence by J. J. McNeal and L. P. Alexander
farms to Moxley near the Kentucky River on the
Owen county side thereof , and the said road shall
be designated as Project No. - and be entitled to
and receive all the benefits of such laws as are now,
or may be hereafter in effect with the relation to the
Primary Road System of this State .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

leading from a point in the Gallatin and Hopkinsville road

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That Section 4356T - 5 of Article 2 , Chapter 110 of
Kentucky Statutes , 1922 Edition , relating to public
roads , be amended by adding and inserting the fol-
lowing project number the road leading from
a point in the Gallatin and Hopkinsville road at or
near Anderson Switch on the L. & N. Railroad in
Todd County , running in a southerly direction to
the Guthrie and Russellville highway, known as
U. S. Highway No. 81 , a distance of about four
miles .
This route when established to be designated as
Project No. and to be entitled to and receive
all the benefits relating to the Primary System of
roads and highways . The State Highway Commis-
sion will have said route surveyed and located .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

CHAPTER 495 .
AN ACT establishing as a part of the primary system of roads
and highways of the Commonwealth of Kentucky a road
leading from a point on U. S. Highway No. 27 near J. C. An-
derson's Store in Strunk , McCreary County , Kentucky , via the
Everett Wood farm to a point near the E. P. Trammel farm .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That there is hereby established as a part
of the primary system of State Highways of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky a road from a point on
U. S. Highway No. 27 near J. C. Anderson's store
to a point near the E. P. Trammell farm on the road
from Bradley Kidd's farm to the Kentucky -Ten-
nessee line, via the Everett Wood farm .
§ 2. The construction , maintenance , supervision
and control of said road shall be subject to and con-
trolled by all the terms and conditions set out and
provided for in Chapter 17 of the Acts of the Gen-
eral Assembly of 1920 , establishing and creating a
primary system of state highways, the same as if
the said road had been named in said original act
as one of the projects of the said primary system
of state highways .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

and Highways of the Commonwealth of Kentucky .

CHAPTER 549 .
CONCURRENT RESOLUTION memoralizing Congress , That it
is the sense of the members of the Kentucky Legislative
General Assembly, the Senate and House concurring, that the
Government of the United States should perform its solemn
promise and place American agriculture on the basis of
equality with other industries by providing an adequate sys-
tem of credit, and that adequate legislation to that end should
be adopted at the earliest possible date .
WHEREAS , unless immediate relief is given , hundreds of
thousands of farmers will lose their farms and their homes
and millions more will be forced into our cities and villages ,
and the army of unemployed will necessarily increase to
alarming proportions ; and
WHEREAS , the price of agriculture products during recent
years has in fact been far below the cost of production ; and
WHEREAS there is no adequate way of refinancing existing
agricultural indebtedness and the farmers are at the mercy
of their mortgagees and creditors throughout this State and
nation ; and
WHEREAS , Senate Bill 457 , introduced in the Senate of the
United States by Senator Lynn J. Frazier of North Dakota ,
provides for the liquidating and refinancing of agricultural
indebtedness and provides for a reduced rate of interest for
the same through the Federal Farm Loan System and the
Federal Reserve Bank System ; and
WHEREAS , the provisions of this bill will have a vital effect
upon the agricultural industry of the State of Kentucky ; and
WHEREAS , at the present time many loans relating to the
agricultural industry should bear a reduced rate of interest ;
and-
WHEREAS , agriculture is the basic industry of this country
and there can be no sound business prosperity until agricul-
ture is put on an equality with other industries : Now, there-
fore ,

WHEREAS , the building of a power dam is being contemplated

Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That it is the sense of the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky that the building of
such a power dam will be of great and lasting bene-

Be it Further Resolved that the true and proper

see River about forty miles south of Paducah , Ken-

Be it Further Resolved that each member of Con-

entries.ocr_text:
1008 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
WHEREAS , it is the policy of the President and the Congress
of the United States to increase the spending power of the
Nation , and the citizens thereof, through various agencies
established by the Congress , and for this purpose , the expen-
diture of large sums from public funds has heretofore been
authorized by Congress ; and ,
WHEREAS , no better way is known for the accomplishment of
this purpose than the payment in cash of the face value of the
said Adjusted Compensation Certificates at the present time,
which, while serving the purpose of paying off an existing
government indebtedness , will serve to circulate cash in every
community , and city of the land no matter how small or how
great , thereby increasing the spending power of the people
in every community , town and city ; and ,-
WHEREAS , by doing this , the Government will recognize a just
obligation to its former soldiers , and bring happiness and
some degree of prosperity to millions of soldier homes ;
Therefore be it Resolved by the Senate of Ken-
tucky , the House of Representatives concurring
therein :
Section One . That the President of the United
States , the Congress thereof , and especially the
Senators and Representatives from Kentucky be and
they are hereby respectfully memorialized , peti-
tioned and urged to use their best efforts and vote
for the passing of a bill now pending in Congress
providing for the immediate payment of the Ad-
justed Compensation Certificates due the World
War Veterans .
Section Two . The Clerk of the Senate is hereby
directed to send a certified copy of this Resolution
to the President of the United States , to the Clerk of
the Senate and of the House of Representatives of
the Congress of the United States , and a copy to
each Senator and member of the House of Rep-
resentatives from Kentucky .
Approved March 6, 1934.

WHEREAS , an oceanway to the mid -continent , besides con-
tributing importantly to national efficiency , is absolutely es
sential to place the interior of the United States on a world
competitive basis ;
Therefore , Section 1, Be it resolved by the House
cf Representatives of the State of Kentucky , the
Senate concurring therein :
That the 1934 session of the General Assembly ,
hereby memoralizes the Senate of the United States
for the speedy commencement of construction of the
Great Lakes -St . Lawrence seaway project , and
urgently requests the members of the United States
Senate and the Senators from Kentucky in particu-
lar, that they not only support immediate ratifica-
tion of the said seaway but that they use every legiti-
mate effort to impress upon their colleagues the
need for lifting the competitive position of mid-
western agriculture and industries in world trade ,
and as a major part in the President's public works
program for the relief of the unemployment .
Section 2. The Clerk of the House of Representa-
tives is hereby instructed to send copies of this
resolution to each member of the Senate of the
Congress of the United States .
Approved January 23, 1934.

CHAPTER 570 1017
Therefore , be it resolved by the House of Repre-
sentatives of the Commonwealth of Kentucky , the
Senate concurring therein :
That the Commonwealth of Kentucky do partici-
pate in the celebration of the Two Hundredth Anni-
versary of the Birth of Daniel Boone and that for the
purpose of preparing and executing a plan or plans
of such celebration a State Commission of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky is hereby established , the
same to be known as the Deniel Boone Bicentennial
Commission , and to be composed of 15 citizens of
the Commonwealth of Kentucky appointed by the
Governor of the Commonwealth of Kentucky , with
said governor an ex-officio member of the same .
Said Commission shall serve without compensation
and shall be empowered and authorized to raise
funds for said celebration and the participation
therein by subscriptions, donations , contributions ,
and other legal means , within and without the
boundaries of the Commonwealth of Kentucky . All
other existing commissions and agencies of the said
Commonwealth of Kentucky which are qualified and
prepared to assist in the said Bicentennial Celebra-
tion are hereby given power to aid the said Commis-
sion in its work . Nothing in this resolution shall be
construed as confining the celebration of the said bi-
centennial solely to the confines of the Common-
wealth of Kentucky . Said commission shall choose
'a Chairman and Secretary from their number and
shall keep a record of their proceedings .
Approved January 30 , 1934 ,
Ruby Laffoon, Governor of Kentucky .

WHEREAS , on the 6th day of October , 1932 , F. Daniel , of
Paintsville , Kentucky , whilst traveling upon the Mayo Trail

lights , no warning or signal of any kind being given or to
warn the public of any danger when approaching said truck ,
and as a result of said truck being so parked the said F.
Daniel was injured , said truck being the property of the
State of Kentucky and being used and operated at the time
by the employees of the State Highway Commission in the
performance of their duties , and
WHEREAS , his chest was broken , crushed and bruised , legs
cut and lacerated , and he was otherwise seriously , painfully
and permanently injured , disabling and incapacitating him
from manual labor for the remainder of his life , and
WHEREAS , said F. Daniel is now 51 years of age and is unable
because of said injuries , to do manual labor, and was at the
time of said injuries earning $300.00 per month , and was re-
quired to expend the sum of $1,000.00 for medical attention ,
hospital bills , etc. , and
WHEREAS , he has sustained these serious and permanent in-
juries by reason of the alleged carelessness and negligence
of the employees of the State Highway Commission in the
conduct of the State's business , therefore,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That F. Daniel be , and he is hereby authorized and
permitted to sue the Commonwealth of Kentucky
and the State Highway Commission , or either, in the
Circuit Court of the county of his residence for such
damages as he may have suffered , if any , by reason
of any injury received by him through the careless-
ness or negligence of the State Highway Commis ..
sion , its agents , or employees . Said suit shall be for
any amount , not exceeding the sum of Six Thousand
( $6,000.00 ) Dollars , and in the event any judgment
is recovered by said Daniel in said suit for injuries
to himself , or same is compromised or settled , same
shall be paid by the Auditor of Public Accounts by
warrant drawn on the State Treasurer , and paid
out of the General Fund .

birthplace of Ollie James in Crittenden County , to be used
for a State park .
WHEREAS , Ollie James is now regarded as one of the nation's
ablest statesmen and one of Kentucky's noblest sons ; and
WHEREAS , he was born in Crittenden County, of native Ken-
tucky parents , and the house in which he was born is still
standing and in good repair ; and
WHEREAS , the Commonwealth of Kentucky may be able to
secure the said birthplace of Ollie James for posterity by
creating there a State park,
Now be it resolved by the General Assembly of
the Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That a committee of five persons be appointed by
the Governor of the Commonwealth to investigate
the desirability and advisability of securing the
birthplace of Ollie James for a State park .
Be It Further Resolved , That after said investi-
gation shall have been completed , the said commit-
tee shall make a report to the Governor of the Com-
monwealth and the State Park Commission .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

1038 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That William Cox be , and he is , hereby authorized
and permitted to sue the Commonwealth of Ken-
tucky and the State Highway Commission , or either ,
in the Wayne Circuit Court , the County of the resi-
dence of said William Cox for damages for the loss
of earning power, physical suffering , and for hos-
pital and medical bills of the said William Cox ,
which were all brought about and caused by the neg-
ligence of the employee of the State Highway Com-
mission. Said suit shall be for any amount not ex-
ceeding the sum of six thousand ( $ 6,000.00 ) dollars ,
and in the event any judgment is recovered by said
William Cox in said suit for injuries to himself , or
same is compromised or settled , said judgment shall
be paid by the Auditor of Public Accounts by war-
rant drawn on the State Treasurer and paid out of
the General Fund .
Either party to said suit may appeal from any
judgment which may be entered therein as in any
other civil suit, and the case may be settled and ad-
justed with the consent and approval of the At-
torney General of Kentucky in the same way as any
other civil case.
Approved March 19, 1934 .

CHAPTER 590 .
A CONCURRENT RESOLUTION declaring the name of the
bridge across the Ohio River in Henderson County to be the
"Memorial Bridge" and dedicated to the memory of James
Audubon .
WHEREAS , the State of Indiana through its legislature has re-
solved that the highway bridge crossing the Ohio River be-
tween Evansville , Indiana , and Henderson , Kentucky , shall
be named in memory of John James Audubon , world-famed
ornithologist ; and
WHEREAS , this action is especially appropriate and fitting be-
cause the region on both sides of the Ohio River surround-
ing the site of the new bridge was the scene of Audubon's
principal works , from which he gained his greatest fame , and
includes ground which he once owned , and which he roamed
in his study of the bird life of that section and in quest of rare
specimens during the years of one thousand eight hundred
ten to one thousand eight hundred twenty ; and

WHEREAS , it has been suggested that in due course the low-
lands on each side of the river established as a federal bird
sanctuary as a further memorial to Audubon , since this was
the region in which he worked , and since the land is ad-
mirably suited for this purpose ; and
WHEREAS , Kentucky have not before suitably recognized
their obligation to the memory of this great man , although
the Federal Government has commemorated his deeds by
naming the snow -capped peaks in Boulder County, Colorado ,
Mt. Audubon , the city of New Orleans has named its largest
park , Audubon Park ; a beautiful monument has been erected
in Trinity Church, New York, and a bust of his likeness was
one of the first to be placed in the Hall of Fame ; and
WHEREAS , it will be to the great advantage of these states ,
the immediate surrounding community at this point, and the
bridge project itself , to dedicate this bridge to the memory
of John James Audubon ;
Therefore , Section One , Be it resolved by the
House of Representatives of the General Assembly ,
of the State of Kentucky , the Senate concurring :
That the General Assembly of the State of Ken-
tucky hereby declare the highway bridge crossing
the Ohio River in Henderson County , Kentucky,
shall be known as the " Audubon Memorial Bridge , '
and directs that officials and employees of the state
shall take whatever steps are necessary to establish
such name for said bridge .
Approved March 19, 1934 .

entries.ocr_text:
CHAPTER 591 1049
executive responsibility of the Governor and make it possible
for him to be Governor in fact instead of in name only , by en-
abling him to really govern and to demand and obtain real
efficiency and economy in the Government . Such a law has
been passed . It reduces administrative departments and agen-
cies of the State Government from seventy -nine to twenty-four ;
it abolishes many useless offices ; it simplifies the Executive
Department ; it creates an Executive Cabinet composed of cer-
tain departmental heads . This law will save large sums of
money by reason of economics which can be enforced by virtue
thereof .
2. The Budget and Financial Administration Act . The people
long have sought the enactment of a real budgetary control law
and a comprehensive and workable financial code . Such a law
has been enacted . It provides for an adequate , unified and inte-
grated system of financial and business administration of the
State Government embracing budgeting , accounting , pre -audit-
ing, treasury administration , post -auditing and fiscal reporting .
The code provides control of income and expenditures , insures
against future treasury deficits , requires all expenditures to be
within the income of the State and prevents any unit of the
Government from spending one dollar, more than its appropria-
tion.
3. Educational Code . It required courage and vision for this
Legislature to over -ride certain prejudices in order to enact
the Educational Code designed to simplify and regulate the
school system of Kentucky , to the end that equal educational
opportunities may be given all of the children of the State ; a
law which will be beneficial to the children of the present and
future generations . The new School Code may be justly called
"A Chapter of Freedom from Ignorance for the Childhood of Ken-
tucky," recognizing and providing , as it does , for the best mod-
ern methods in education and giving the children of the State
qualified teachers .
4. Three Million Dollar Farm Land and Real Estate Reduc-
tion . This General Assembly has done a thing which for years
representatives , loyal to the best interests of the people , have
sought in vain to accomplish . It has taken a large part of the
burden of taxation from off the shoulders of farmers and other
real estate owners , by reducing the tax on real estate from
thirty cents to five cents on the hundred dollars , thereby saving
in excess of three million dollars annually to this class of citi-
zens who through all the history of Kentucky have been forced
to bear an outrageously unjust proportion of the burden of
taxation .
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1934, p. 1110

1050 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
5. One Million Two Hundred and Fifty Thousand Dollar Auto
License Reduction . A law has been enacted reducing the cost
of automobile licenses more than one -third , thereby saving the
automobile owners One Million Two Hundred and Fifty Thou-
sand Dollars annually .
6. Two Hundred and Fifty Thousand Dollar Truck License
Reduction . A law reducing truck licenses has been passed
thereby saving truck owners more than Two Hundred and Fifty
Thousand Dollars .
7. Reorganization of County Government . Several laws have
been enacted dealing with the reorganization of County govern-
ment which will result in the saving of millions of dollars to
the taxpayers of the State .
8. Uniform Narcotic Law . A uniform narcotic law was
passed which without increased expense of enforcement , places
Kentucky in the vanguard of civilized states in the reduction
of this great evil .
9. Amendments Workmen's Compensation Law . Certain
amendments to the Workmen's Compensation law were enacted
which insure the erection in Kentucky of several large factories ,
furnishing employment to many laborers ; and ,
WHEREAS , this Legislature has broken all precedents in the
number of days it has actually held sessions for the consider-
ation of legislation , no time having been wasted by junkets
or other frivolities and even during interims when the Legis-
lature was not in actual session , its committees were actively
engaged in giving conscientious attention to their duties , yet
the time required for the careful study , preparation, and pas-
sage of the beneficial laws enumerated above , as well as of
the much other remedial legislation enacted has made it im-
possible for the members of this General Assembly to give
proper consideration to the necessary and all important
legislation having to do with fiscal affairs , yet such study as
has been made of these important matters reveals the fact
that many of the members of the General Assembly came to
Frankfort with an entirely erroneous idea of the true financial
condition of the State , having been deceived by propaganda
broadcast by designing and corrupt interests , opposed to the
present administration . The real facts have not been revealed
to the people , but by garbled statements , the publishing of
half truths and by the use of every possible artful wile , these
interests have deliberately sought to make the people believe
things which are false and untrue about their government and
its officials . However, personal investigation has undeceived

CHAPTER 591 1051
us . We no longer take as true , the things we are told or
which we read in newspapers , and
WHEREAS , it appears from such investigation , that the follow-
ing conditions exist :
1. The penal and charitable institutions of the State are anti-
quated , dilapidated and wholly inadequate for the humane care
of their inmates ; many buildings have gone without paint or
repairs for years ; heating plants and water systems are suffer-
ing from lack of attention ; roofs are leaking ; the continued neg-
lect of these absolutely essential repairs entail an annual loss
of at east Two Hundred and Fifty Thousand ( $ 250,000.00 ) Dol-
lars ; Staffs of these institutions are too small to give other
than mere custodial care ; confronted with rising costs for all
supplies and lowered income , with populations of the institu-
tions the largest in their history , due to the depression , causing
all of them to be crowded far beyond their capacity , we declare
an extreme emergency to exist , which those of us who can be
touched by emotions of sympathy and a desire to relieve suffer-
ing and distress , intend to relieve by requiring all the people
(not merely the farmer ) to pay their proportionate part of the
necessary cost thereof.
2. The excellent Children's Home Society at Lyndon is
caring for more than twice as many children as ever before ,
with twenty per cent . less income .
3. The Kentucky School for the Blind has an increase in
students of more than one hundred per cent .
4. Health nurses and sanitary inspectors for communities
must be discontinued unless the necessary revenue is provided .
5. Kentucky must continue to lag in the rear in regard to the
proper care of those suffering from the curable disease of tu-
berculosis unless adequate funds are provided .
6. Crippled children must be neglected if the necessary re-
duction in appropriations are made to bring this important
agency within the State's income .
7. The Mammoth Cave National Park appropriation so much
desired by all Kentuckians must be postponed unless the neces-
sary revenue is forthcoming .
8. Over and above all else , however , our schools must suffer
unless and until the people of Kentucky demand that the mem-
bers of the Legislature display the necessary courage and vision
to secure for Kentuckians , the educational facilities which can
only be obtained through adequate revenue laws . If this is not
done , then the $ 780,000.00 teachers ' salary equalization fund
appropriation must go and common school teachers can hope

9. The State has been required to give warrants in payment
for necessary supplies , amounting to approximately Five Mil-
lion Dollars annually , and since State warrants have been sell-
ing , on an average , of around eighty cents on the dollar , some
selling as low as seventy cents , those selling supplies add ap-
proximately twenty per cent to the amount of their bills to take
care of the discount on warrants , the State thereby suffering
a loss of approximately One Million Dollars annually , because it
cannot pay cash .
10. Investigations reveal the further fact that the receipts
from all sources of revenue have fallen from $37,336,561.00 in
1931 to $29,580,748.00 in 1933 , an annual decrease in receipts
of $ 7,755,813.00 . This large decrease is due to a number of
causes , among them being the reduction in the assessed valu-
ation of real and personal property ; the reduction in intangible
property ; the loss on collections of fines and forfeitures (men
choosing to go to jail rather than pay their fines ) ; the repudia-
tion of replevin bonds ; the great reduction in the amount of
fees received ; the reduction in taxes on bank deposits ; the in-
ability to pay taxes (land sold for taxes having increased over
three hundred per cent ) ; the loss of receipts from prison labor
caused by reason of a Federal law , this loss alone amounting
to approximately Three Hundred Thousand Dollars .
11. All of these factors uniting have already caused the
large reductions in receipts referred to , and when to these are

occasioned by recently enacted laws , several million dollars

It highlights significant decreases in the common school fund, the state's inability to pay cash for supplies leading to discounts on warrants, and a general decline in revenue from various sources. The text attributes these issues to factors such as reduced property valuations, lower fine collections, inability to pay taxes, and loss of prison labor revenue due to federal law. It also mentions recent laws impacting auto and truck licenses and criticizes propaganda that falsely claims the administration has not practiced economy.
classification.reasoning: The provided text discusses the financial state of Kentucky in 1934, detailing budget cuts, revenue shortfalls, and the impact of economic conditions on state services like schools. There is no mention of race, racial segregation, or any discriminatory practices against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is identified as 'session_laws' based on the header 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY' and the year 1934.

CHAPTER 591 1053
smoke screen , the attempt to force the farmer and real estate
owner to continue to bear alone the burden of taxation . We
have found this propaganda wholly false and untrue , and that
it is being published and broadcast by designing , biased and
highly prejudiced persons and newspapers and by paid lobby-
ists representing a highly organized and well financed mi-
nority . The truth is, that the present administration of Ken-
tucky has reduced the expenses of operating the State from
$40,082,946.00 in 1931 to $34,311,538.00 in 1933 , making a re-
duction in the cost of the Government of $5,771,408.00 per
annum . Notwithstanding this enormous reduction , in an edi-
torial appearing recently in a certain highly biased and preju-
diced newspaper , the reduction in the cost of government
made by a number of other States was pointed out and
praised , yet this was done in such an artful and deceptive
manner as to make the people of Kentucky believe that no
economy had been practiced in Kentucky , when the writer
well knew the facts , but did not choose to tell the whole truth
as we have found it . The facts are that the reduction in the
cost of the Government of Kentucky was twice as large as
that of any State named in the editorial other than California ,
while the income of all the States named was from three to
ten times more than that of Kentucky , thus making our re-
duction in expenditures proportionately the largest of any
State in the Union . We find the expenditures from the gen-
eral fund alone were $3,184,000.00 less in 1933 than in 1931 ,
but unfortunately the receipts of the general expenditure fund
in 1933 were $2,843,000.00 less than in 1931 , yet these truths
are being deliberately withheld from the public for ulterior
purposes , and
WHEREAS , due to the conditions hereinbefore set out , it ap-
pearing to our satisfaction that the expenses of the govern-
ment of Kentucky have been so greatly cut that it would be
thoroughly unwise to reduce them any further and that this
with the continuing decrease in receipts makes it absolutely
essential , in order that the State government may properly
function , for the members of this General Assembly to enact
legislation for the purpose of raising the necessary revenue
upon which to operate the government , balance its budget
and bring its warrants back to par , therefore ,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
1. That it is the sense of the members of this
General Assembly that an extra -ordinary session of

classification.summary: This statute is a resolution by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky addressing state finances. It refutes claims of excessive spending and highlights reductions in state expenses between 1931 and 1933. The resolution argues that due to significant cuts in government expenses and a decrease in receipts, it is essential to enact legislation to raise necessary revenue for the state government to function properly, balance its budget, and bring its warrants back to par. It also suggests the possibility of an extraordinary session.

classification.title: AMEND CONCURRENT HOUSE RESOLUTION Number 79
classification.summary: This resolution amends a previous concurrent house resolution by striking out specific language and substituting new language. The new language establishes a commission of 9 members of the legislature to investigate income tax and other revenue sources, study revenue-raising methods used by other states, and survey the financial needs of state government departments, boards, commissions, and agencies. The goal is to determine the actual amount of money required to properly conduct state affairs on a business basis and to balance the budget.
